searchIndex.title: Introduction
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework that brings the Next.js developer experience to the Python ecosystem. You write your server logic in Python, your UI in React, and ship them together i
searchIndex.searchText: Introduction Pyxle is a Pythonfirst fullstack web framework that brings the Next.js developer experience to the Python ecosystem. You write your server logic in Python, your UI in React, and ship them together in a single file — no separate API layer, no second service, no glue code between them. Current version: 0.4.0 (beta) One file, two languages A file colocates a Python loader (and mutations) with the React component that renders its data. The compiler splits them apart at build time, so the Python runs on the server and the JSX runs in the browser — but you author them as one unit, because they describe one thing: a single route. The loader's return value is the component's prop. There's no , no API route to wire up, no type contract to keep in sync — the seam between frontend and backend simply isn't there. Why Pyxle No API plumbing. A loader feeds props straight into your component; an is an endpoint you call with instead of handwriting + a route. Real React. any React library and use it directly — Pyxle renders genuine React 18 with serverside rendering and hydration, not a Python wrapper around a component library. Convention over configuration. Filebased routing, automatic SSR, and zero config for the common cases. Run and you're shipping. Batteries includable. Official plugins for database, auth, and email, plus hooks for middleware, edge caching, and your own integrations. AIfirst DX. Predictable patterns, strong types, and clear errors make Pyxle one of the easiest frameworks for an AI coding agent to ship a whole feature in a single pass. See Pyxle for AI coding agents. Where to go next New here? Start with Installation, then build your first app in Quick Start. Want the mental model? Read Files, Routing, and Data Loading. Coming from another framework? See how Pyxle stacks up in Pyxle vs. other frameworks. Shipping to production? Read the Deployment guide. Curious how it works inside? Take the tour in the Architecture handbook. What's new Pyxle 0.4.0 adds edge caching, hardened production errors, and faster static serving. See the full Changelog for release notes. GitHub: github.com/pyxledev/pyxle Install: PyPI: pypi.org/project/pyxleframework

searchIndex.title: Quick Start
searchIndex.description: Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes.
searchIndex.searchText: Quick Start Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes. 1. Scaffold a new project This creates a directory with a complete starter project. 2. Install dependencies This runs both and . You can also run them separately: lists your app's runtime dependencies (Starlette, Uvicorn, HTTPX). Pyxle itself comes from the in step 1. 3. Start the dev server Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser. You should see the Pyxle starter page — a centered card showing the framework version, server time, and a link to edit . Tailwind compiles automatically because the scaffold ships with — PostCSS runs as part of the Vite pipeline, so there's nothing separate to start. What just happened? When you ran , the framework: 1. Compiled split the Python server code from the React JSX 2. Started Vite the JavaScript bundler that serves your React components with hot reload 3. Started Starlette the Python ASGI server that handles routing, SSR, and API requests 4. Ran the loader fetched data on the server and passed it as props to React 5. Rendered HTML on the server sent fullyrendered HTML to the browser (SSR) 6. Hydrated on the client React took over the serverrendered HTML for interactivity 4. Make a change Open in your editor. Change the returned by : Save the file. The browser reloads automatically with your updated message. 5. Check your routes This prints the route table derived from your directory: 6. Validate your project This validates syntax, checks your config file, and reports any issues. searchIndex.title: .pyxl Files
searchIndex.description: A `.pyxl` file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file -- your data fetching and your UI live together.
searchIndex.searchText: Files A file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file your data fetching and your UI live together. Anatomy of a file A file has two sections: The compiler finds the boundary automatically: it grows the largest region that parses as valid Python (using Python's own AST), and treats whatever isn't valid Python as JSX. There are no separator comments or directives to write. The Python section The Python section runs entirely on the server. It can: Import modules any Python package available in your environment Define a loader an async function that fetches data for the component Define mutations async functions callable from the client Rules for the Python section One loader per file. The loader receives a Starlette and must return a JSONserializable dict. Multiple functions are allowed. Each becomes a callable endpoint. The function must be . Pyxle enforces this at compile time. Use any Python imports. Standard / statements stay in the Python section because they parse as valid Python. (A JavaScript import such as isn't valid Python, so it is correctly treated as part of the JSX section — the split is by what parses, not by the leading keyword.) The and decorators are available globally you do not need to import them (the compiler injects the import automatically). An optional directive caches the rendered page for seconds (serverside, with incremental regeneration). See Caching. The JSX section The JSX section is a standard React component. It runs on both the server (for SSR) and the client (for hydration and interactivity). Rules for the JSX section Must have a default export. The default export is the page component. Receives as props. The prop contains whatever the loader returned. If there is no loader, is an empty object . Can import from . This gives you , , , , , , , , and . Can import from . Any npm package in your is available. Cannot import Python code. The Python and JSX sections are compiled separately. Must be a pure function of its props. No data fetching, I/O, secrets, Node builtins, side effects, or nondeterministic values (, ) in the render body — load in , mutate in , and put browseronly or effectful code in / event handlers / . See Component purity below. Component purity A component's render is a pure function of its props: given the same , it produces the same HTML, with no side effects. Whatever runs while the page renders on the server must be deterministic. Don't, in the render body: fetch data, query a database, or touch the filesystem / network — that's the loader's job read secrets or import Node builtins (, , , , …) mutate state or cause other side effects use nondeterministic values like , , or — they differ between the server render and the client and cause hydration mismatches Do instead: You want to… Use load data for the page a loader (runs before render; the result arrives as ) change data an , called with or run code after mount, or anything browseronly , an event handler, or This isn't a stylistic preference — purity is what makes a component hydrationsafe (server and client render the same thing), testable (render is a function you can assert on), and fast (no hidden perrender I/O). It also keeps rendering portable to faster, sandboxed SSR backends in the future. Effects and event handlers are exempt — they don't run during the server render. Purity is about the render pass, not the whole component. Controlling the document Use the component from to control what goes in the document : Anything inside is extracted during SSR and inlined into the document . It supports dynamic values via normal JSX interpolation: Head values are automatically sanitised to prevent XSS injection angle brackets inside text are escaped, event handler attributes are stripped, and URLs are neutralised. Note: Pyxle also supports a lowerlevel Python variable for the rare cases where you want fully static head metadata extracted at compile time. For everyday pages, prefer the component. See Head Management for both mechanisms and when to use each. A complete example JSXonly files If a page has no server logic, you can write a JSXonly file: Next steps Learn how files map to URLs: Routing Add data fetching: Data Loading

searchIndex.title: Routing
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses **file-based routing**. The file structure inside your `pages/` directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed.
searchIndex.searchText: Routing Pyxle uses filebased routing. The file structure inside your directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed. Basic routes Every file in becomes a route: File URL The filename is special it maps to the parent directory's path. Dynamic segments Wrap a filename segment in square brackets to create a dynamic route parameter: File URL Pattern Example Access dynamic parameters in your loader via : Parameter name rules Hyphens in parameter names become underscores: The parameter name is sanitised to be a valid Python identifier Catchall routes Use to match any number of path segments: File URL Pattern Example Matches , The parameter captures the entire remaining path as a string: Optional catchall routes Use (double brackets) to match the parent path and any subpaths: File URL Pattern Example Matches and , , This creates two routes: one for the exact parent path and one for the catchall. Route groups Wrap a directory name in parentheses to exclude it from the URL: Route groups let you organise files by concern without affecting URLs. API routes Python files under become API endpoints: File URL API routes are standard Python files (not ). See API Routes for details. Viewing your route table Run to see all registered routes: Special files These filenames have special meaning and do not create their own routes: File Purpose Wraps sibling and descendant pages in a shared layout Like layout but resets state on navigation Error boundary for sibling and descendant pages 404 page for the current directory scope See Layouts and Error Handling. Next steps Load data for your pages: Data Loading Wrap pages in shared layouts: Layouts

searchIndex.title: Data Loading
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses `@server` decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the Re
searchIndex.searchText: Data Loading Pyxle uses decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the React component. Basic loader The function: 1. Receives a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/requests/) object 2. Must be (enforced at compile time) 3. Must return a JSONserializable 4. The return value is available as in the React component Accessing request data The parameter is a full Starlette . You have access to: Returning status codes Return a tuple of to set the HTTP status: Caching the render For a page whose content is the same for every visitor and changes rarely, return a envelope instead of a plain dict. Pyxle caches the rendered HTML and serves it back without rerunning the loader, refreshing it in the background once it goes stale: is the props your component receives; is the freshness window in seconds. Only do this for pages that render no peruser data — a cached render is shared with every visitor. See Caching for invalidation, incremental regeneration, and the full contract. Error handling in loaders Raise to trigger the nearest error boundary: When is raised: 1. Pyxle searches up the directory tree for the nearest 2. If found, it renders the error boundary with the error context as props 3. If not found, a default error page is shown See Error Handling for full details. Using external APIs Loaders can call any Python code databases, APIs, file systems: Calling blocking libraries Loaders are async and run on the server's event loop. Calling a blocking library directly — a sync database driver, , a sync SDK — stalls every other request handled by that worker while the call runs. Wrap blocking calls in so they execute on a worker thread: If the blocking work is a database read, keep one persistent connection per worker thread (for example in a ) instead of connecting per request connection setup usually costs more than the query itself. For API routes there is a second option: a plain is dispatched through Starlette's threadpool automatically. See Sync endpoints and blocking calls. Pages without loaders If a page has no function, is an empty object: How it works 1. A request hits a page route (e.g., ) 2. Pyxle imports the compiled Python module and runs the function 3. The return value is serialised to JSON 4. The React component is rendered on the server with as props 5. The full HTML is sent to the browser 6. React hydrates the page on the client, using the same props embedded in the HTML By default the loader runs on every request. To cache the rendered page and skip the loader on later requests, return a envelope see Caching. Next steps Mutate data with server actions: Server Actions Handle errors gracefully: Error Handling

searchIndex.title: Server Actions
searchIndex.description: Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to `@server` loaders -- loaders read data, actions write data.
searchIndex.searchText: Server Actions Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to loaders loaders read data, actions write data. Defining an action Use the decorator on an async function in your file: The function: Must be Receives a Starlette object Must return a JSONserializable Can only be called via request Multiple actions can exist in the same file alongside a loader. Calling actions from React Using the component The simplest way to call an action from a form. collects the inputs, posts them to the action, and exposes / callbacks: props: Prop Type Description Name of the function Override which page the action belongs to (defaults to current page) Called with response data on success Called with the error message and, for a validation failure, the perfield errors () — otherwise Reset form fields after success (default: ) Using the hook For programmatic calls (not form submissions), use the hook: returns an async function with attached state: Property Type Description while the request is in flight Error message on failure, otherwise Perfield validation errors from the last failed submit (a ), or . Cleared when a new request starts Last successful response data Options: Option Type Description Override which page the action belongs to Called immediately before the request (for optimistic updates) Error handling in actions Raise to return a structured error response: Since 0.3.0, is autoimported by the compiler for any file that declares at least one . No explicit import is required. A userdefined class (or an existing import) is respected and takes precedence. The client receives: Validating request bodies with Pydantic Reading and pulling fields out by hand means writing the same "is this present, is it the right type, is it in range" checks in every action. Pyxle can do that for you: annotate a parameter with a Pydantic model and the dispatcher parses, coerces, and validates the request body before your action runs. Your function only executes with a valid, fullytyped model. The body parameter: Is the first parameter (after ) annotated with a Pydantic subclass. The name is up to you (, , , …). May be / for an optional body. Is parsed from the JSON request body. submissions (which post form fields) validate the same way. Pydantic is an optional dependency. Install it with the extra: If an action declares a Pydantic body but Pydantic isn't installed, the request fails with a clear (and the dev server logs why) — actions without a model parameter are unaffected. What the client receives on a validation failure When the body fails validation, the action is not called. The client gets a response with a toplevel map — field path to a list of messages: Field paths are dotted for nested models () and indexed for list items (). Showing field errors in the UI Both and surface the map so you can render messages next to each input. With , read (or the reactive ): With , the field map is the second argument to : Raising validation errors yourself For checks Pydantic can't express — uniqueness, crossfield rules, anything that needs a database — raise . It produces the same shape, so the client handles modellevel and handrolled errors identically: is autoimported alongside in any file that declares an . See the Runtime API reference. Exporting an OpenAPI schema Because the body models are real Pydantic models, Pyxle can generate an OpenAPI 3.1 document describing every action's request body straight from your code: See the CLI reference for options. Invalidating client caches after a mutation Pyxle's client router caches loader payloads (for 2 minutes by default, or the route's configured TTL) so back/forward navigation is instant. A mutation that changes data the user might navigate back to should invalidate the stale route so the next visit refetches: Pyxle attaches an header to the response. and honour the header automatically — neither the calling component nor the page component needs to know about it. For purely clientdriven invalidation (no server roundtrip), use [](../reference/clientapi.md#invalidateurl) from . Running work after the response To do work without making the client wait — send an email, emit a webhook — schedule it on . Pyxle runs it after the response is sent: For a single task you can return the shorthand instead. For fireandforget work that isn't tied to this response (including from loaders), use [](../guides/backgroundtasks.md#fireandforgetworkpyxletasks). See the Background Tasks guide. How actions are routed Each function gets an automatic endpoint: For example, in is available at: You do not need to know these URLs and resolve them automatically. Calling an action directly (scripts and tests) App code never needs the URL, but a script or test can POST to it directly. Send a JSON body; the response is the action's return dict (with ), or when it raises an : CSRF protection is on by default, so a raw like this is rejected with unless you either exempt the action path in [](../reference/configuration.md#csrf) () or send a valid header matching the cookie. CSRF protection Actions are protected by CSRF middleware by default. The component and hook handle token management automatically. See Security for details. Next steps Wrap pages in layouts: Layouts Handle errors with boundaries: Error Handling

searchIndex.title: Layouts
searchIndex.description: Layouts wrap pages in shared UI -- navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations.
searchIndex.searchText: Layouts Layouts wrap pages in shared UI navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations. Creating a layout Add a file to any directory inside . It wraps all pages in that directory and its subdirectories: A layout is a React component that receives : Slots Layouts can export a object and a function for passing additional content from pages: Nesting layouts Layouts nest automatically. If both and exist, a page at is wrapped by both: Inner layouts are rendered inside outer layouts. The root layout is always the outermost wrapper. Templates A file works like a layout but resets component state on every navigation. Use templates when you want a fresh React component tree for each page in the group: Templates are useful for authentication flows, wizards, or any section where you want form state and scroll position to reset when moving between pages. Layout vs template Behaviour Wraps child pages Yes Yes Preserves state on navigation Yes No (remounts) Can nest Yes Yes Typical use Nav bars, sidebars Auth flows, wizards Layout data loaders A layout (or template) can declare its own loader, just like a page. This is the clean way to load data the layout itself needs on every page it wraps a nav bar, a signedin user/session banner, the current theme, the framework version, etc. without repeating it in every page's loader. The layout component receives its loader's result on the prop exactly like a page receives its loader's data. Details: The loader runs once per request, before the page renders, and must be (same rules as a page loader). In a nesting chain, every layout/template loader runs and their results are merged into one dict (on a key conflict, the outermost layout wins). A layout without a loader receives the wrapped page's instead, so existing JSXonly layouts are unchanged. The merged layout data is also exposed to the page component as a prop, if a page ever needs to read what its layouts loaded. How it works When Pyxle compiles your pages, it: 1. Walks up from each page to the root, collecting and files 2. Generates a composed wrapper module that nests them in the correct order 3. At render time, the page component is passed as to the innermost layout Next steps Style your layouts with Tailwind: Styling Add navigation between pages: Client Components

searchIndex.title: Pyxle vs. other frameworks
searchIndex.description: Pyxle isn't the only way to build a full-stack app in Python, and it isn't the right tool for every job. This page is an honest map of the landscape — what each alternative is genuinely great at, and
searchIndex.searchText: Pyxle vs. other frameworks Pyxle isn't the only way to build a fullstack app in Python, and it isn't the right tool for every job. This page is an honest map of the landscape — what each alternative is genuinely great at, and when you should reach for it instead of Pyxle. The short version: Pyxle's niche is real React UI plus real Python server logic, in one file and one service, with conventions predictable enough that an AI coding agent can ship a whole feature in a single pass. If you never want to write JavaScript, if you're building a data dashboard, or if you need a decademature ecosystem at largeteam scale today, one of the tools below is probably a better fit — and we'll say so plainly. At a glance UI you write Languages Services to run Real React ecosystem Sweet spot Pyxle React / JSX Python + JS one ✅ full React apps, solo & small teams, AIassisted dev Next.js + FastAPI React / JSX Python + TS two ✅ full Large teams, maximum ecosystem & scale Reflex Python Python one ⚠️ wrapped Teams that would rather not touch JS Django Templates (or + React SPA) Python (+ JS) one (or two) ❌ (or twoservice) Content / CRUD apps, mature batteries NiceGUI Python Python one ❌ (Vue under the hood) Internal tools & dashboards, fast Streamlit Python Python one ❌ Data / ML dashboards & demos "Real React ecosystem" means you can any React library and use it directly, rather than through a frameworkspecific wrapper. Pyxle vs. Next.js + FastAPI This is the stack Pyxle most directly replaces: a Next.js (React/TypeScript) frontend talking to a FastAPI (Python) backend over an API. It's the gold standard for a reason — both halves are mature, battletested, and have enormous ecosystems, and the separation scales cleanly to large teams who want a hard frontend/backend boundary. The cost is everything in the seam: two languages, two runtimes, two deploys, a handwritten API contract, CORS, and the type drift between your Pydantic models and your TypeScript types. Pyxle collapses that seam. A loader's return value is your component's props; an is the endpoint, called with instead of . One file, one service, no glue. Use Next.js + FastAPI instead when you have a large team that benefits from a strict frontend/backend split, you need the maturity and scale guarantees of the two mostproven tools in their categories, or you're already invested in that stack. Reach for Pyxle when the twoservice tax is pure overhead for your team size and you'd rather ship one thing. Pyxle vs. Reflex Reflex lets you build the entire app — UI included — in pure Python; it compiles your Python components down to a React/Next.js app under the hood. For a team that never wants to write JavaScript, that's a genuinely compelling promise, and Reflex executes it well. The trade is indirection: you're expressing your UI through Reflex's component abstractions rather than React itself, so reaching for an arbitrary React library or a bit of custom client behavior means going through (or around) that layer. Pyxle makes the opposite bet: you write real React/JSX, so the entire npm React ecosystem is available with no wrapper — but you do write some JavaScript. Use Reflex instead when "zero JavaScript, ever" is a hard requirement for your team. Reach for Pyxle when you want the real React ecosystem and are comfortable writing JSX next to your Python. Pyxle vs. Django Django is the batteriesincluded Python web framework: a superb ORM, the legendary admin, auth, migrations, and a vast, mature ecosystem. For serverrendered, content and CRUDheavy applications it's still one of the best choices in any language. But Django's native UI story is serverrendered templates. The moment you want a rich React frontend, you're back to a twoservice setup (Django REST Framework + a separate React SPA) — the very split Pyxle exists to avoid. Use Django instead when you want a mature ORM and admin out of the box, your UI is well served by serverrendered templates, or you're building something contentheavy where its ecosystem shines. Reach for Pyxle when you specifically want a React UI without bolting on a second service to serve it. Pyxle vs. NiceGUI NiceGUI is a delightful way to build web UIs in pure Python (on top of Vue/Quasar and FastAPI). For internal tools, control panels, and dashboards, it's fast to write and gets you a working interface in minutes. Like Reflex, the UI is defined in Python rather than React, which is exactly what makes it quick for tooling — and exactly why it's a less natural fit for a polished, publicfacing product where you want full control over the React component tree. Use NiceGUI instead for internal tools and dashboards you want to stand up quickly in Python. Reach for Pyxle for publicfacing apps where you want a real, handauthored React frontend. Pyxle vs. Streamlit Streamlit is phenomenal at what it's built for: turning a Python data script into an interactive app. For data exploration, ML demos, and analytics dashboards, nothing gets you there faster. Its execution model — the script reruns top to bottom on every interaction — is the source of that magic, and also the reason it's not aimed at generalpurpose web apps with custom routing, finegrained interactivity, and a bespoke UI. Use Streamlit instead for data and ML dashboards and quick analytical apps. Reach for Pyxle when you're building a generalpurpose web application rather than a data tool. So which should you choose? Choose Pyxle when you want a real React frontend and a Python backend without running two services, you value predictable conventions (for yourself or your AI agent), and you're happy being an early adopter of a framework that's moving quickly. Choose something else when: You never want to write JavaScript → Reflex or NiceGUI You're building a data or ML dashboard → Streamlit You need a mature, proven ecosystem at largeteam scale today → Next.js + FastAPI or Django Your app is contentheavy CRUD and you want an admin + ORM out of the box → Django A fair caveat: Pyxle is 0.x and evolving — APIs may still shift before 1.0. If you need rocksolid stability for something missioncritical shipping next week, pick a mature option or wait for 1.0. But if you're starting something new and the onefile Python + React workflow sounds like how you want to build, that's exactly who Pyxle is for. Curious about the AIagent angle specifically? See Pyxle for AI coding agents. Ready to try it? The Quick Start takes about five minutes.

searchIndex.title: Styling
searchIndex.description: Pyxle ships with Tailwind CSS out of the box. The recommended way to load your stylesheets is to **import them from a JSX module** so Vite can compile, bundle, and content-hash them — exactly like it
searchIndex.searchText: Styling Pyxle ships with Tailwind CSS out of the box. The recommended way to load your stylesheets is to import them from a JSX module so Vite can compile, bundle, and contenthash them — exactly like it does for JavaScript modules. This means you never have to handbump a query string after a deploy to invalidate stale browser caches. Recommended: Vitemanaged CSS (autohashed) When your project has a file alongside the Tailwind config, Pyxle delegates CSS processing to Vite + PostCSS. The standalone Tailwind CLI watcher is automatically skipped (you'll see a clear log line on dev server start), and any CSS file you import from a JSX module flows through PostCSS, gets a content hash, and is listed in the Vite manifest under your page entry. The SSR template reads that manifest on every request and emits automatically. The hash changes only when the CSS source changes, so users with old caches always see the latest styles after a deploy. Setup A new Pyxle project has everything wired by default. If you're upgrading an existing project, here's the full setup: 1. Add to your project root: 2. Make sure and are in : 3. Import your stylesheet from a JSX module. The cleanest place is the root so every route picks it up via the layout wrapper: 4. Delete any manual tags from your blocks. Vite owns the link now. 5. (Optional) remove the nowinert and scripts from . Only relevant when upgrading an older project that defined them — the current scaffold's ships just //, so a fresh project has nothing to remove. When PostCSS is configured, Pyxle skips the standalone watcher, so those scripts never run on their own anyway. Verifying the setup Start the dev server: You should see a log line like: Open the page in your browser. Tailwind classes work as before. In dev mode Vite serves the CSS through its own runtime (with HMR), so changes hotreload instantly. There's a brief flash of unstyled content on the very first paint in dev — this is a Vite quirk and does not happen in production builds. For production: Inspect and you'll see each route's array pointing at contenthashed assets: Two consecutive builds with no source changes produce the same hash. A build after editing (or any source it depends on) produces a different hash. Cachebusting is automatic. Tailwind config The Tailwind config still lives at (or , , ). PostCSS calls it during compilation, so your globs still control which files Tailwind scans for class names: Dark mode The scaffold includes a theme toggle script that reads from and respects . Toggle the class on to switch themes: Use Tailwind's modifier for darkmode styles: Importing CSS from any JSX module The same pattern works for any CSS file, not just Tailwind. Componentscoped stylesheets, thirdparty CSS modules, or vanilla CSS files all work: Vite resolves the specifier relative to the importer, runs it through PostCSS, hashes the output, and lists it under the page's manifest entry. The SSR template emits a for every CSS file the page transitively imports. Global stylesheets (configdriven) For CSS that should be inlined on every page (no separate request, no hashing — embedded directly in the SSR HTML), register it in : Paths are relative to the project root. Global styles are loaded in order and inlined as tags, so they work even before JavaScript loads. Use this for tiny critical CSS — for anything substantial, prefer the JSXimport path so Vite can hash and cache it properly. Global scripts Register JavaScript files loaded on every page: Legacy: standalone Tailwind CLI watcher If you don't want Vitemanaged CSS (e.g. you're integrating with an external build pipeline), you can use the standalone Tailwind CLI watcher instead. Skip the file — its presence is what tells Pyxle to defer to Vite — and add the watcher scripts to : will autostart the watcher whenever it detects a Tailwind config and no PostCSS config. You can disable it explicitly with: Then link the compiled output manually: Tradeoff: this path has no automatic cachebusting. If you deploy a CSS change and a user has the old file cached, they'll see stale styles until their cache expires. Workarounds ( query strings, handrolled fingerprints) are inevitable. The Vitemanaged path avoids all of this. CSSinJS Any CSSinJS library that works with React 18 and SSR should work with Pyxle. Install it via npm and import it in your JSX section. Next steps Manage document head elements: Head Management Add scripts with loading strategies: Client Components
headings: Importing CSS from any JSX module

searchIndex.title: Head Management
searchIndex.description: Pyxle offers two ways to control the document `<head>`: the `<Head>` component (**recommended**) and the `HEAD` Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — bu
searchIndex.searchText: Head Management Pyxle offers two ways to control the document : the component (recommended) and the Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — but we recommend for almost every real page. TL;DR — use the component That's the pattern you'll use for the vast majority of pages. It reads like React, interpolates props naturally, and lives next to the body markup that depends on the same data. Why is the recommended approach It's just JSX. If you know React, you already know how to use it. No new concepts, no callablevsstring rules to memorise. Dynamic content is effortless. You can interpolate , map over arrays, use conditionals, extract to subcomponents — everything JSX lets you do with the body works in the head too. Colocation. Your sits right next to the that uses the same data. Refactoring one updates the other in the same diff. Works in nested components. Any component in your render tree can contribute head elements. A component can set its own ; an wrapper can add . Plays well with layouts. Layouts can set defaults with their own , and pages override them automatically through Pyxle's deduplication rules. Familiar to developers coming from other frameworks. The API is intentionally similar to Next.js's , Remix's export, and React Helmet. The component Import it from and drop it anywhere in your component tree. Its children become elements in the document : The component: Renders nothing in the DOM (it returns ). During SSR, Pyxle extracts its children at compile time and registers them as head elements for the response. Works in any component, including nested ones. A reusable component can inject its own head metadata. Supports head elements: , , , , . Anything outside this allowlist is dropped by the head sanitiser — notably , which is rejected because a stray can rewrite every relative URL on the page (an XSS/redirection vector). Normalises multipart children since 0.3.0. compiles to multiple children — — which React warns about. joins string and number children into a single text node so the warning is silenced and the rendered HTML is unchanged. You don't need template literals or ${name} — My Blog\ workarounds. Multiple blocks in one tree You can use multiple times — it's not a singleton. Elements from all blocks are collected and merged: This lets you put head contributions close to the code that decides them, without having to lift that logic all the way up to the page root. Using in reusable components Head contributions from any rendered component get merged into the final document: This is the idiomatic way to build SEO presets, thirdparty tracking tags, and theme toggles. The variable (lowerlevel alternative) files can also define a variable in the Python section. This was Pyxle's original head mechanism and still works; the parser extracts it at compile time, before React is involved. For head content that depends on loader data, use a callable: The callable receives the loader's return value as its argument and must return a string or list of strings. When to prefer the variable There are a few narrow cases where the variable is a better fit than : 1. Pages with no React component. A pure APIlike page that returns minimal HTML and wants a static without any clientside JavaScript. 2. Absolute hot paths where the few microseconds of skipping React's head capture matter, and the content is fully static. In practice this matters for approximately no one. 3. You deliberately want the head to be decoupled from the render tree — for example, if a page's head is determined by something the component doesn't need to know about. Unless one of these applies, reach for . XSS safety Both children and strings are parsed and rebuilt through a sanitising allowlist before they reach the document: Only , , , , and are permitted in the head — anything else (, , …) is dropped Every attribute value is HTMLescaped, so a quote inside interpolated data cannot break out of its attribute — building // values from loader data (as in the callable above) is safe Angle brackets (, ) inside text are escaped, and any markup injected after a closing is discarded Event handler attributes (, , etc.) are stripped , , and URLs in // attributes are neutralised is rejected One deliberate exception: the text content of inline and elements is treated as trusted author code and preserved verbatim — never interpolate usersupplied data into inline script or style content. Note that and elements are reserialised selfclosing (), so the output markup may differ cosmetically from the input string. This protects against XSS when interpolating userprovided data into head elements. You should still escape user input as a best practice. Layouts and precedence When multiple sources define the same head element, Pyxle deduplicates them. Later sources override earlier ones. Precedence order (lowest to highest) 1. Layout blocks and layout variable 2. Page variable 3. Page blocks Within the same tier, deeper nesting wins (a in a child component overrides a in a parent component). Example: layout defaults + page overrides When the route renders, the layout's and its description are both overridden by the page's values. The favicon survives because the page doesn't define one. Deduplication rules Element Deduplicated by Tag name (only one title allowed) The attribute The attribute Always one charset Only one canonical + combination The attribute Elements with The key value Everything else Not deduplicated (all instances kept) Manual deduplication keys Use to control deduplication for custom elements that don't have a natural identity attribute: If a layout and a page both define an element with the same , the higherpriority source wins. This is useful for tag managers, featureflag bootstrap scripts, or A/B testing snippets. Default title If no element is provided by any source, Pyxle inserts a default: Next steps Add thirdparty scripts: Client Components Build JSON APIs: API Routes
headings: When to prefer the HEAD variable

searchIndex.title: Caching
searchIndex.description: By default a page's `@server` loader runs on **every request** and the page is rendered fresh each time. For pages whose content is the same for everyone and changes rarely — a marketing page, a docs
searchIndex.searchText: Caching By default a page's loader runs on every request and the page is rendered fresh each time. For pages whose content is the same for everyone and changes rarely — a marketing page, a docs article, a blog post — that work is wasted. Pyxle's page cache stores the rendered HTML and serves it back without rerunning the loader or the SSR render, then refreshes it in the background when it goes stale. The one rule: only cache pages that render no peruser data. A cached render is shared byteforbyte with every visitor. If a page embeds a loggedin user's name, a CSRF token, or anything requestspecific, do not cache it — you would serve one user's page to another. Caching is always optin, exactly so this stays your deliberate choice. Making a page cacheable Return a envelope from your loader instead of a plain dict. is the props your component receives (exactly as before); is how many seconds the cached render stays fresh. That's it. The first request renders and stores the page; requests within the next 60 seconds are served from the cache — no loader, no Node render. The envelope is recognised only in its exact twokey shape ( and , nothing else). A normal loader that happens to return keys named or is never mistaken for a cache directive. Pages without a loader A page with no loader can still opt into caching with a modulelevel directive in its Python section: is the freshness window in seconds, exactly as in the loader envelope ( means "serve cached but rerender every request"). It is validated at compile time — a nonnumeric or negative value is a build error. If a page declares both a loader envelope and a directive, the loader's wins. Incremental regeneration (stalewhilerevalidate) When a cached page passes its window, the next request still gets the cached (stale) bytes immediately — no one waits for a rerender — and a single background rerender refreshes the cache for everyone after it. This is incremental static regeneration (ISR): fast responses, freshenough content, and never a thundering herd (only one refresh runs per page at a time, even under load). is valid and means "serve the cached copy but rerender on every request" — useful when you want to absorb bursts without ever serving content older than one render. Static prerendering () Pages with no loader and no dynamic route parameters render the same HTML for everyone, every time. renders them once at build time and stores the result, so the first request after a deploy is already a cache hit — no cold SSR render, even for the very first visitor. This prerenders every loaderless, nondynamic page into ; on startup warms its page cache from that directory. Pages with a loader (or a route) are skipped — they still render live and cache at runtime as before. Prerendered entries have no expiry until you the route. With the default inmemory backend they are rewarmed from the new on every restart; with a shared file/redis backend, warmed copies persist in that store, so if a later deploy stops passing (or adds a loader to a previouslystatic route), flush the cache or invalidate those routes — replacing does not clear them. Invalidating the cache When the underlying data changes — you publish a post, edit a page — purge the cached render so the next request rerenders immediately instead of waiting out the window: purges one route's cached render. Returns if something was cached, otherwise — safe to call either way. purges every cached render. How it interacts with the edge cache A route listed in your [](../reference/configuration.md) block (the edge cache, which sets for a CDN) is also served from the serverside page cache automatically, using that same TTL — you don't need to repeat yourself. When both apply, a loader's wins over the edge . Cached page responses carry a strong , so a conditional request () gets a , and an response header reports , , or for debugging. Where the cache lives The cache is enabled automatically for production serves () and disabled in so a cached render never hides an edit while you work. Choose where rendered HTML is stored with the environment variable: Stores in Use when (default) bounded inprocess memory (LRU) single process, or any deploy where perworker caches are fine local disk () one host, multiple workers sharing a cache that survives restarts shared Redis () multiple hosts, or crossworker invalidation nothing — caching disabled you want it off in production The inmemory backend is bounded by entry count (, default 512) and total body bytes (, default 64 MiB) with LRU eviction, so it never grows without limit. The Redis backend needs the optional extra: . Invalidation and workers. With the inmemory or file backend under , each worker keeps its own store, so reaches the worker that handled the action; entries still expire everywhere on their own via . The Redis backend is shared across every worker and host, so an invalidation fans out to all of them — choose it when you need crossworker purges. When not to cache Pages that show the signedin user's data, a peruser CSRF token, or anything that varies by request. Leave these as plain loaders (), and they are never cached. Pages that must always reflect the absolute latest data with zero staleness — use a plain loader, or plus on every write. Query strings: the cache keys on the route path only, so a request that carries a query string (, ) is always served live — never cached and never served a cached entry. A page whose content varies by query is therefore never collapsed onto one shared render. Next steps Load data: Data Loading Mutate data: Server Actions Configure the edge cache: Configuration
headings: How it interacts with the edge cache

searchIndex.title: Streaming SSR
searchIndex.description: By default Pyxle renders a page to its complete HTML string and then sends it. For a page with a slow part — a section that waits on a third-party API, a heavy below-the-fold widget — the visitor star
searchIndex.searchText: Streaming SSR By default Pyxle renders a page to its complete HTML string and then sends it. For a page with a slow part — a section that waits on a thirdparty API, a heavy belowthefold widget — the visitor stares at a blank screen until the whole page is ready. Streaming SSR sends the page in pieces. Pyxle flushes the fast part (the "shell") immediately, so the browser can paint it, and streams the slow parts in as they become ready. Timetofirstbyte drops to the time it takes to render the shell instead of the whole page. Streaming is built on React 18's and is optin: a page streams only when it uses a boundary. Every other page keeps the buffered render, unchanged. Opting in with Wrap the slow part of your page in a boundary and give it a . The fallback renders into the shell and streams immediately; the boundary's real content streams in when it resolves. The compiler detects the at build time and marks the page as streamable — there's no configuration and no flag to set. A page with no is never affected. works the way it does in any React 18 app: a child suspends by throwing a promise (via , a call, or your own promisethrowing data source), and React shows the until it resolves. Pyxle's loader still runs first and passes its result as props, exactly as for a buffered page — streaming governs how the rendered page is delivered, not how the loader runs. Routelevel loading states with A file declares a routelevel loading state — the React component it exports becomes the fallback for the whole route, the way Next.js's does: Pyxle wraps the page in — on the server (so the loading state streams as the shell) and on the client (so hydration matches). The nearest wins, walking up the directory tree exactly like [](errorhandling.md). It composes with a page's own inner : an inner boundary handles its own subtree, and anything it doesn't catch bubbles up to the shell. A is compiled but never routable on its own. A only shows if the render actually suspends. Because the loader runs before the component renders, a page whose data comes entirely from the loader has its props ready by render time and never suspends — so the fallback never appears, and the full page streams at once. The loading state shows only when the render itself suspends: a child using , , or a thrown promise. This is the honest consequence of Pyxle's loaderfirst model; if you want a loading state for slow loader data, that data isn't what defers. A should not declare a — like any streamed fallback it renders before the head is finalized, and on a clientside navigation the head is updated before the fallback renders, so a fallback's is ignored. Hydration Nothing changes about hydration. The browser hydrates the same component it always did; React 18 reconciles the streamed markup (including the parts that arrived after the shell) natively. A streaming page is hydrated exactly like a buffered one. Hydration is selective: React hydrates the shell (and makes its interactive parts live) without waiting for a boundary to finish — an interactive control above the boundary responds to input while the boundary is still resolving, and each boundary hydrates as its content arrives. Faster timetofirstbyte The point of streaming is that the browser receives — and can paint — the shell before the slow boundary is ready. For a page whose boundary takes, say, 600 ms to resolve, the shell's first byte arrives in tens of milliseconds rather than after the full 600 ms a buffered render would wait. The slower the boundary, the bigger the win; a page with no slow boundary gains nothing, which is exactly why streaming is optin. When a page does not stream Streaming is deliberately narrow. A page falls back to the buffered render when: It has no boundary. There's nothing to defer, so there's no shell to flush early. It is publicly cacheable — a loader envelope, a directive, or an edge config entry (see Caching). A cacheable render has to be materialised in full so it can be stored and given an ; streaming it would buy nothing, so cacheable routes always render buffered. Streaming helps the dynamic, perrequest pages that can't be cached. The server is running without an SSR worker pool. Streaming needs the pool's multiframe transport. runs the pool by default. A streamed response is always — it is a perrequest render and is never shared between visitors. The head is static while streaming Because the document is flushed before the component renders (that's what makes the shell fast), only the static head is available to a streamed page: the variable (including a callable evaluated from loader data) and blocks declared in your JSX or layout. A element registered during render — i.e. returned from a component as it renders — arrives too late to reach the alreadyflushed head and is omitted from a streamed page. Put meta tags a streamed page needs in the variable, not in a runtimerendered . This only affects pages that opt into streaming; buffered pages merge runtime exactly as before. Errors If the render fails before the first byte is sent — an error while producing the shell — Pyxle does not emit a halfwritten document. It falls back to the nearest [](errorhandling.md) boundary (or the sanitized error page), exactly like a buffered render. Once the shell has flushed, an error inside a boundary is handled by React: it streams the boundary's fallback and recovers on the client.
headings: The head is static while streaming

searchIndex.title: WebSockets
searchIndex.description: A Pyxle page can serve a **WebSocket** alongside its HTML. Add a module-level `async def websocket(ws)` to a page's Python section and Pyxle registers a WebSocket route at the page's path — the same f
searchIndex.searchText: WebSockets A Pyxle page can serve a WebSocket alongside its HTML. Add a modulelevel to a page's Python section and Pyxle registers a WebSocket route at the page's path — the same file serves both the rendered page over HTTP and a live connection over WS. Open and you get the page; the client hook upgrades the same path to a WebSocket. Two browsers in exchange messages in real time; is a separate room. The handler Detection is by convention, not a decorator: a modulescope coroutine named is the handler. (This matches how modules already expose a callable, and keeps free of new symbols.) It must be at module scope — a or a is a compile error (almost always a mistyped handler). Its first argument is the Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/websockets/) — name it whatever you like (, , …); it is not a . Dynamic segments resolve into (e.g. for ), exactly like a page loader. You call yourself, then read with / and write with / . The handler owns the full connection lifecycle. A page WebSocket handler runs without middleware — no auth population, no CSRF, no route hooks. Starlette runs HTTP middleware only for scope. See Authentication below. Channels (pub/sub) provides a small broadcast layer so you don't handroll connection bookkeeping. subscribes the connection to a named channel for the life of the block (unsubscribing on disconnect), and gives you a handle to to everyone in it: A published message reaches every subscriber, including the sender — a chat UI that doesn't want to echo the sender's own messages filters them clientside. are delivered as binary frames, as text, anything else as JSON. The broker lives on (one per process). For a custom backend, implement the protocol ( / / ) and set your own. Multiworker caveat. The default lives in one process. Under , each worker has its own broker — a client on worker 1 never receives a message published on worker 2 (Pyxle logs a warning at startup when this combination is detected). For crossworker realtime, use a shared backend (Redis pub/sub) or stickysession routing at the load balancer. The default is correct for and singleworker . Authentication in a WebSocket handler Because the auth middleware never runs for a WebSocket upgrade, is not available. Resolve the session yourself with , which reads the session cookie off the handshake and returns the user (or ): It returns (doing zero work) when [](../plugins/pyxleauth.md) isn't installed or no session cookie is present. Origin checking. CSRF doesn't apply to a WebSocket upgrade, so a crosssite origin check is the equivalent guard against a hostile page opening a socket with the victim's cookie. Enforce it when the socket carries privileged state: The client hook connects from the browser with autoreconnect, JSON parsing, and connection state. It never connects during SSR and reconnects with exponential backoff (capped, with jitter). Returns Type Description Connection state Send a string asis, or JSONencode anything else; if not open The most recent received message (JSONparsed when possible) The last error message Options: , , (default ), (default ). A relative is resolved against the current origin with the matching scheme ( on ); an absolute / URL passes through. See also API routes — modules can export a callable too (and an for HTTP on the same path). pyxleauth — the session the WS auth helper reads. Deployment — multicore serving and the broker caveat.

searchIndex.title: API Routes
searchIndex.description: Files under `pages/api/` are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not `.pyxl`) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses.
searchIndex.searchText: API Routes Files under are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not ) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses. Basic API route Create . An API module exports an callable that receives the Starlette and returns a response: handles every HTTP method bound to the route. This responds to : HTTP methods receives every method bound to the route. Branch on to handle more than one: For multimethod endpoints with automatic handling, use an class (below) — Starlette dispatches each request to the matching //… method and rejects the rest. Using HTTPEndpoint classes For more structure, use Starlette's : Sync endpoints and blocking calls can also be a plain synchronous function. Pyxle dispatches sync endpoints through Starlette's threadpool, so a blocking body — a database driver, a sync SDK — occupies a worker thread instead of freezing the event loop: The same applies to sync //… methods on classes — Starlette threadpools those natively. Inside an endpoint, never call blocking libraries directly — that stalls every request on the worker's event loop. Either make the endpoint sync (above) or wrap the call: For submillisecond calls the syncendpoint form is usually faster — one threadpool hop per request instead of a hop per wrapped call. Note: route hooks (and the default API policies) wrap function endpoints. classes are dispatched natively by Starlette and bypass route hooks — the same rationale as WebSocket routes. WebSocket endpoints Since 0.3.0, an API module can export to register a WebSocket handler at the same path. The file can export both (HTTP) and — they bind to the same URL and Pyxle dispatches based on the protocol of the incoming request. Client side: You can also export a Starlette subclass for multimethod dispatch: Notes: WebSocket handlers run outside the HTTP routehooks pipeline — hooks wrap requesttoresponse callables and the WS lifecycle doesn't match that shape. Authenticate, ratelimit, and log inside the handler body. CSRF doesn't apply to WebSocket upgrades. Enforce your own origin / session checks in before . Dynamic API routes Use the same bracket syntax as page routes: Reading request bodies Error responses Return appropriate HTTP status codes: API routes vs server actions Feature API routes Server actions File location Inside files HTTP methods Any (GET, POST, PUT, etc.) POST only Response format Any Starlette Response JSON dict Called from Anywhere (curl, fetch, etc.) or CSRF protection On for POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE¹ Enabled by default Use case Public APIs, webhooks, integrations Form submissions, mutations ¹ CSRF runs appwide, so a statechanging API request (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) must carry the doublesubmit token by default — same as any other route. A public webhook or thirdparty integration that can't send the token must list its path prefix in [](security.md). Safe methods (GET/HEAD/OPTIONS) are never checked. Next steps Add middleware to your routes: Middleware Protect routes with CSRF: Security

searchIndex.title: Background Tasks
searchIndex.description: Some work shouldn't make the user wait: sending a welcome email, emitting a webhook, warming a cache. Pyxle gives you two ways to run it off the request's critical path — pick by *when* the work shoul
searchIndex.searchText: Background Tasks Some work shouldn't make the user wait: sending a welcome email, emitting a webhook, warming a cache. Pyxle gives you two ways to run it off the request's critical path — pick by when the work should run. You want… Use Runs Work tied to a response (after this replies) After the response is sent Fireandforget work from anywhere (loader, action, API) On a background worker pool Both run inprocess on the app's event loop. For work that must survive a restart or run exactly once across machines, reach for a real job queue — see below. Postresponse work in an Inside an , is a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/background/). Add tasks to it and Pyxle runs them after the response is sent, so the client isn't kept waiting: accepts a coroutine function or a plain function (run in a threadpool). The task runs after the response — a failure in it can't change what the client already received, so log inside the task if you need to know it ran. The shorthand For a single task, you can return it from the action instead of touching : The value is ; Pyxle strips the key from the response body and schedules to run after the response. For more than one task, use . Fireandforget work — When the work isn't tied to a particular response — a loader that wants to refresh something, an API route emitting an event — enqueue it on the app's inprocess task queue: schedules on a small pool of background workers (coroutine functions are awaited; plain callables run in a thread, so a blocking SDK call won't stall the loop). It returns immediately. A task that raises is logged — it never takes a worker down. The queue is bounded; under sustained overload raises rather than growing without limit. The queue starts and stops with the app, so works inside any request. Called outside a running app it raises . Multiworker caveat. The inprocess queue lives in one process, so under each worker has its own queue, and queued work is lost if that worker restarts. It's the same tradeoff as the inprocess WebSocket broker. For durability or exactlyonce semantics across workers, use a real job queue. Production job queues Pyxle's inprocess tasks are deliberately small — perfect for "send an email after signup," not for a videotranscoding pipeline. When you need retries, scheduling, durability, or workers on separate machines, hand off to a dedicated queue. They slot in cleanly because an is just async Python: Celery — the most common choice. Define a , then call from an action. Run as a separate process. ARQ — asyncnative, Redisbacked. from an action; ARQ's worker runs it. A natural fit since Pyxle handlers are already async. Dramatiq — then . In every case the pattern is the same: the action enqueues a job and returns; the external worker does the heavy lifting. Pyxle doesn't wrap these — use their own client directly so you keep their full feature set. See also Server Actions Runtime API →

searchIndex.title: Middleware
searchIndex.description: Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: **application-level middleware** that wraps every request, and **route-level hooks** that run only for specific route types.
searchIndex.searchText: Middleware Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: applicationlevel middleware that wraps every request, and routelevel hooks that run only for specific route types. Applicationlevel middleware Add Starlettecompatible middleware classes to your config: Each entry is a string in format. The class must be a standard Starlette middleware or subclass. Writing a middleware Middleware is applied in the order listed in config. The first middleware in the list is the outermost wrapper. Routelevel hooks Route hooks run before and after specific route handlers. Configure them per route type: Note: Route hooks wrap function endpoints ( in ) and page handlers. classes are dispatched natively by Starlette and bypass route hooks — as do WebSocket handlers — so auth or ratelimiting for classbased endpoints must live in the endpoint methods themselves or in applicationlevel middleware. Writing a route hook (function style) The function receives three arguments: 1. a with metadata about the matched route 2. the Starlette 3. an async callable that invokes the next handler Writing a route hook (class style) RouteContext The object provides metadata about the matched route: Property Type Description Route type URL path pattern File path relative to project root Absolute file path on disk Python import key Hash of the compiled route module — changes when the source changes, stable across reloads Whether the page has a loader Rendered markup registered by the page/layout (SSR only) HTTP methods the route handles is a frozen dataclass — fields are readonly. A shorthand returns a JSONfriendly view (keys camelCased, paths as POSIX strings) that Pyxle attaches to for downstream middleware. Builtin hooks Pyxle applies two default hooks: adds route info to returns 405 for disallowed API methods Middleware execution order From outermost to innermost: 1. Custom middleware (from config) 2. CORS middleware (if configured) 3. CSRF middleware (if enabled) 4. Vite proxy (dev mode) 5. Static file serving 6. Route hooks (from config) — skipped for classes and WebSocket handlers, which Starlette dispatches natively 7. Page/API handler Next steps Configure environment variables: Environment Variables Add CORS and CSRF: Security

searchIndex.title: Plugins
searchIndex.description: Pyxle plugins are reusable pieces of functionality — database layers, auth systems, storage adapters — that a host app composes via `pyxle.config.json` rather than hand-wiring in its entry point. The
searchIndex.searchText: Plugins Pyxle plugins are reusable pieces of functionality — database layers, auth systems, storage adapters — that a host app composes via rather than handwiring in its entry point. The design is deliberately Djangoinspired: declare what you want, and the framework handles discovery, startup order, and lifecycle. Status (0.4.0). This guide covers the Phase A plugin surface: lifecycle hooks, named services, and middleware contribution. Page contribution (plugins shipping their own pages that get merged into the host's route tree — the "pyxleauth ships a default page" use case) is a Phase B followup. Everything documented here is stable; the additions in Phase B will be strictly additive. TL;DR Declare plugins in : Pyxle imports each plugin, calls its hook inside the ASGI lifespan, and gives your app three ways to reach the services plugins register. Pick whichever reads best — they all resolve to the same object. All three coexist. The pluginprovided helpers (option 1) are preferred for app code because they typeannotate the return value; option 2 is there for adhoc access; option 3 is the fundamental mechanism. Config schema Each entry in is either a string or an object: Field Required Default Description ✅ — Userfacing name. Used for servicenamespace identification and error messages. — Python module where the plugin object lives. — Attribute on to fetch. A subclass is instantiated; an instance is used asis. — Perapp config dict forwarded to the plugin instance via . The string form is sugar for . Both sugar and object form work sidebyside. Authoring a plugin Minimum viable plugin A plugin is a regular Python package that exports a subclass (or instance) named : Ship a typed import helper Every plugin should ship a oneliner helper so consumers can import instead of reaching into the registry: Consumer code then writes — typed, short, idiomatic. See the and helpers for realworld examples. Handling plugin settings Settings arrive as a dict from the host app's . The framework doesn't validate the shape — your plugin should. A simple pattern: The "unknown keys" check is important — a typo like would otherwise silently fall through to the default and leave the user wondering why their setting isn't taking effect. Fail loud at startup, not at runtime. Declaring a dependency on another plugin Plugins declare dependencies in two places: 1. In — so pip installs the dep alongside your plugin. 2. In at servicelookup time — plus a clear error if the required service isn't present (which means the host app forgot to list the dependency in , or listed them in the wrong order). Phase B will introduce declarative dependencies so the framework can toposort plugins for you. For now, order is manual. Environment variables Plugins that read environment variables should document the contract clearly in their README. There's no frameworklevel envvar declaration yet — Phase B adds for style validation. Testing a plugin The cleanest pattern mirrors the framework's own tests: drive the plugin through its real public API ( → → ) rather than unittesting internals. Tests look the same whether you're writing a plugin or consuming one. Packaging + publishing Ship your plugin as a normal Python package: and list in a host app's — that's the whole distribution story. No entry points or registration dance. Lifecycle Startup failures abort the ASGI app immediately — a plugin that can't reach its database should raise, and Pyxle propagates it so Starlette refuses to serve traffic. Shutdown failures are logged but don't abort teardown of the remaining plugins. Consuming services from your app Option 1: pluginprovided import helpers (preferred) Official plugins expose a typed helper function that reaches into the registry for you: This is the closest Pyxle gets to Django's pattern. Thirdparty plugins should ship similar helpers — it's one extra line of code for a much nicer consumer experience. Option 2: generic shortcut For services that don't have a typed helper (or adhoc access to the registry): raises when the name isn't registered and no default is provided. Error messages list every registered name, so typos are obvious at first request rather than silently returning . Option 3: long form via the request Useful inside middleware that already has the and wants to stay contextpure (no modulelevel state reads): When to use which App loaders and actions → option 1 (or 2 if no helper exists). Short and typed. Middleware, route hooks, requestscoped helpers → option 3. Avoids the modulelevel state read, which makes the middleware easier to test in isolation. CLI scripts or tests calling plugin code outside an ASGI context → install the context manually with and then any of the three forms work. Middleware contribution A plugin can declare ASGI middleware it wants appended to the host app's stack: Each entry is : Import string — either (Starletteconvention form) or (plain dotted form). Both resolve the same way. Options — a dict of keyword arguments passed through as , matching the shape of . Stack position Plugincontributed middleware sits between the host app's (from ) and the Vite proxy. In order from outermost to innermost the stack looks like: A plugin that adds requestid middleware therefore sees requests after CSRF has validated them but before the Pyxle router dispatches, which is usually what you want. Plugins that genuinely need to run before CSRF (e.g. a plugin handling webhooks with a different validation scheme) should register an exempt path via rather than trying to reorder middleware. When to use middleware vs. route hooks Middleware — crosscutting request transforms (request IDs, ratelimit headers, metrics). Runs on every request. Route hooks — perroute decoration (auth check on a subset of routes). Declared in by host apps, not plugins today. A plugin like deliberately doesn't install middleware — it ships a service the host app consumes inside its own loaders. That way apps stay in control of which routes require auth, and the plugin doesn't gate traffic the app owner didn't opt into. Service naming conventions Namespace Owner Each official plugin gets its own prefix. Reserved for the host app's own adhoc registrations. Plugins should document every name they register so consumers know what to ask for. Error handling Condition Exception Bad config entry (missing name, nonlist, etc.) at load time Module can't be imported at startup Attribute is missing / wrong type at startup raises wrapping the original — ASGI startup aborts misses at request time Troubleshooting The package isn't installed, the module path is wrong, or the plugin author forgot to create . Checklist: 1. — is the package actually installed in this interpreter? 2. — does the module import cleanly outside the devserver? 3. If the package uses a nonstandard module layout, set explicitly on the config entry: The package exports a attribute that isn't a subclass. Either the plugin author made a typo or you're pointing at the wrong attribute. Override on the config entry if the plugin uses a nonstandard export name: The listed available names are the full registry snapshot. Common causes: Typo. Compare letterbyletter with the available list. Wrong order. requires before it in the config list. Plugin not installed. The helper import succeeded but the plugin isn't in . Plugin startup raises Look at the wrapped — that's the plugin's real failure (DB not reachable, API key invalid, schema drift, etc). Fix that; the layer is just framing. raises "No active plugin context" in a test You're calling the helper outside an ASGI request. In tests, install a context manually: Changing doesn't take effect Pyxle resolves the plugins list at devserver startup, not perrequest. Restart (or your production server) after editing the plugins list. Limitations (Phase A) Plugins cannot contribute their own pages yet. To ship a default signin page today, the plugin author provides the source string and the host app imports it —

searchIndex.title: Environment Variables
searchIndex.description: Pyxle loads environment variables from `.env` files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and client-side injection.
searchIndex.searchText: Environment Variables Pyxle loads environment variables from files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and clientside injection. files Create files in your project root: Load order Files are loaded in order of increasing precedence. Later files override earlier ones: 1. base defaults 2. or modespecific 3. local overrides (gitignore this) 4. or local mode overrides The mode is when running and when running or . The shell always wins Variables already set in your shell environment are never overwritten by files. This means CI/CD environment variables and Docker env take precedence. Syntax Doublequoted values support escape sequences: , , , , . prefix Variables starting with are injected into clientside JavaScript at build time: Access them in your JSX via : Serveronly variables (without the prefix) are never exposed to the client. They are only available via in your Python code. config overrides These environment variables override settings from : Variable Config field Example or Precedence From lowest to highest: 1. Defaults in Pyxle 2. 3. files 4. environment variables 5. CLI flags (, , etc.) Page cache backend These select where the serverside page cache stores rendered HTML. The default needs no configuration. Variable Meaning (default), , , or inmemory: max entries before LRU eviction (default ) inmemory: max total body bytes (default , 64 MiB) file backend: directory for cache files (required for ) redis backend: connection URL (falls back to ) The cache is active only for production serves (); never caches. The backend needs . Using environment variables in loaders recommendations Next steps Handle errors gracefully: Error Handling Secure your app: Security

LoaderError Raise from a function to trigger the nearest error boundary: LoaderError parameters Parameter Type Default Description (required) Error message shown in the error boundary HTTP status code for the response Additional context passed to the error boundary ActionError Raise from an function to return a structured error to the client: The client receives . ActionError parameters Parameter Type Default Description (required) Error message sent to the client HTTP status code Additional data in the error response Error boundaries () Create an file to catch errors from pages in the same directory and below: An error boundary is a React component that receives the error context as props: Error props The prop contains: Property Type Description The error message HTTP status code Exception class name Additional data (if provided via ) Boundary resolution When an error occurs, Pyxle walks up the directory tree from the page that failed until it finds an : throws 1. Check 2. Check 3. Use default error document Notfound pages () Create a file to customise the 404 page: Like error boundaries, notfound pages follow directory scoping a in handles 404s within . Dev mode error overlay During development (), errors also appear in a browser overlay with: The error message and stack trace Breadcrumbs showing which stage failed (loader, renderer, hydration) File path and line number The overlay communicates via WebSocket and updates in real time as you fix errors.
