searchIndex.title: Security
searchIndex.description: Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box.
searchIndex.searchText: Security Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box. CSRF protection CSRF (CrossSite Request Forgery) protection is enabled by default using a doublesubmit cookie pattern. How it works 1. Pyxle sets a cookie on every response 2. Statechanging requests (, , , ) must echo the cookie value back, either in the header or — for formencoded submissions, such as a post before hydration — in a hidden form field 3. The framework validates that the submitted token matches the cookie using constanttime comparison 4. If they do not match, a response is returned The component and hook handle this automatically. Pyxle buffers at most 1 MB of a formencoded body to find . A larger or body that doesn't carry the header token is rejected with — for large uploads, send the token via the header instead. Configuration Option Type Default Description Enable/disable CSRF Cookie name Header name Set flag (enable in production with HTTPS) attribute (, , or ) Paths exempt from CSRF checks; an entry matches its exact path and anything beneath it at a boundary ( does not exempt ) — see matching details Disabling CSRF Or with the object form: Token validation for custom fetch calls If you make requests outside of or , read the CSRF token from the cookie and include it as a header: If you've customised or , use your configured names instead — Pyxle also injects nondefault names into every page as and , so custom code can read those globals (falling back to / ) rather than hardcoding. Signed cookies and tokens and (importable from ) attach a tamperproof signature to a string and verify it later — the building block for a signed session id, a "remember me" cookie, or a stateless link such as an unsubscribe or passwordreset URL. This is signing, not encryption: the value stays readable; a valid signature only proves it was produced by a holder of the secret. The signature is a full HMACSHA256 digest, compared in constant time. The secret is read from the environment variable, or passed explicitly as . Signing is meaningless without a secret, so a missing one raises rather than returning an unprotected value — it fails closed. Use to namespace signatures by purpose, so a token signed for one flow can't be replayed in another even under the same secret: returns on any signature or format failure. Because a validlysigned empty string is itself falsy, test the result with , not truthiness: CORS configuration Configure CrossOrigin Resource Sharing for API access from other domains: Option Type Default Description Allowed origins (CORS is disabled if empty) Allowed methods Allowed request headers Allow credentials (cookies, auth headers) Preflight cache duration in seconds CORS is disabled by default (no configured). It is only active when you explicitly list allowed origins. Head element sanitisation Dynamic head content (whether from a JSX block or the lowerlevel variable) is automatically sanitised to prevent XSS: Tag allowlist: only , , , , and may appear; any other tag (, , , …) is dropped Attribute escaping: elements are parsed and rebuilt with every attribute value HTMLescaped, so a quote in dynamic data cannot break out of its attribute and inject markup Title text escaping: and inside elements are escaped to and , and markup injected after a closing is discarded Event handler stripping: , , , and all attributes are removed Dangerous URL neutralisation: , , and URLs in , , and attributes are emptied Meta refresh rejected: is dropped as an openredirect vector The text content of inline / head elements is trusted author code and is not sanitised. Never interpolate usersupplied data into inline script or style content. This sanitisation is applied to all head elements from every source — layout blocks, page blocks, and the legacy Python variable. Best practice React already escapes text content by default when you interpolate into JSX, so the component inherits XSS protection automatically: React escapes in the text node, and Pyxle's head sanitiser provides a second layer of defense. You don't need for JSXdriven head content. For the lowerlevel Python variable with usersupplied data, escape explicitly: Security headers In production, configure your reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy, etc.) to add security headers: Environment variable safety Variables without the prefix are serveronly and never appear in client bundles Loader and action return values are serialised to JSON and sent to the client never include secrets in return values Use for secrets and add it to Next steps Deploy to production: Deployment Full config reference: Configuration

searchIndex.title: Observability
searchIndex.description: Pyxle gives every request a **correlation id** and measures how long it takes — on by default, with no setup. Both are cheap enough (a `uuid4` and two `perf_counter` reads, no I/O) to run on every req
searchIndex.searchText: Observability Pyxle gives every request a correlation id and measures how long it takes — on by default, with no setup. Both are cheap enough (a and two reads, no I/O) to run on every request in production. Heavier, exporterstyle observability — structured logs, a Prometheus metrics endpoint, OpenTelemetry tracing — is configured under the same [](../reference/configuration.md#observability) block and stays off until you opt in. Request IDs Every request is assigned a correlation id and the response carries it as the header: The same id is available to your server code on , so you can log it alongside your own messages and tie a userreported error back to a single request: A helper is also exported for code that may run before is populated: Trusting an upstream id By default Pyxle generates a fresh id and ignores any the client sent — echoing a clientsupplied value into your logs is a loginjection and spoofing vector. When Pyxle runs behind a trusted reverse proxy or gateway that already stamps a request id, opt in so the id flows through end to end: Even then, an incoming id is only honoured if it is wellformed (, at most 128 characters); anything else is replaced with a generated id. Custom header name Request timing With enabled (the default), Pyxle records each request's wallclock duration. The measurement is taken at the outermost layer, so it reflects the full time the framework spent on the request (to the point the response starts). This timing feeds the metrics and structuredlogging features; it is the foundation the rest of this guide builds on. Metrics Pyxle keeps an inprocess registry of request, SSRrender, loader, and durations (as fixedbucket histograms) plus pagecache hit/stale/miss counts. Recording is always on and cheap — a handful of integer adds per request, no perrequest allocation, bounded memory. Exposing those metrics is optin (the endpoint reveals internal state). Turn on a Prometheus endpoint: Guard it with a bearer token, and serve it on a custom path, if you like: With a token set, the endpoint requires (compared in constant time). The pagecache metrics require the serverside page cache, which runs under . Multiworker note. Under each worker process keeps its own registry, so the endpoint reports that worker's numbers — every series carries a label. Aggregate across workers at your Prometheus scraper (e.g. ). A shared crossworker registry is planned for a later release. Health probes Pyxle serves two probes for orchestrators (Kubernetes, load balancers): — liveness. Always while the process is up; the body reports , , and a flag. — readiness. once the server has warmed up and every dependency check passes, otherwise . The body lists each check: The dependency checks are fast attribute reads (never a network roundtrip), so the probe stays cheap. Today verifies the SSR worker pool has at least one live worker; point your readiness probe at it so traffic is only routed to a worker that can actually render. Structured logging Turn on a structured access log — one line per request, carrying the method, path, status, duration, and the request's correlation id: Set to (the default) for a humanreadable line instead, and to tune verbosity. The correlation id is bound into a context variable for the duration of the request, so your own log calls within a loader or action carry the same automatically when you log through the logger or your own logger configured the same way. Structured logging works with no extra dependency (a stdlib JSON/console formatter). Installing the optional extra adds structlog for richer rendering: It's off by default so it doesn't disrupt an existing log pipeline. OpenTelemetry tracing For distributed tracing, Pyxle emits OpenTelemetry spans for each request and child spans for the SSR render, loaders, and calls — so a slow page shows up as a trace you can drill into. It's the heaviest integration, so it's a separate optional extra and fully off by default. Install it and turn it on: The exporter endpoint is read from the standard environment variable (so it works with any OTLPcompatible collector). Sampling defaults to 5% so tracing can't swamp a busy server. When the extra isn't installed, spans are a noop with zero perrequest cost (a single boolean check) — but enabling without the extra installed fails loudly at startup rather than silently dropping traces. Tracing is kept entirely optional: a base Pyxle install pulls in no OpenTelemetry packages. Dev dashboard Run to print a live observability panel to the terminal every few seconds — a zerodependency, ataglance view of what your dev server is doing: It reads the same inprocess metrics described above, so it costs nothing on the request path, and it's devonly — there's no equivalent in , where you'd scrape the metrics endpoint instead. Turning it off Requestid and timing are individually toggleable, and a bare disables both: See the configuration reference for every key and default.

searchIndex.title: Deployment
searchIndex.description: Deploy a Pyxle application with `pyxle build` to compile assets and `pyxle serve` to run in production.
searchIndex.searchText: Deployment Deploy a Pyxle application with to compile assets and to run in production. Build for production This: 1. Compiles all files into Python and JSX modules 2. Runs — Vite bundles JS and processes CSS through PostCSS (Tailwind is compiled inpipeline when is present, which is the scaffold default) 3. Outputs production artifacts to the directory Build options Serve in production This starts a production Starlette server without Vite (static assets are served directly): In production mode () the server also compresses responses larger than 500 bytes with gzip automatically — no reverseproxy configuration needed for that. Serve options Flag Default Description Bind address Port number / Server worker processes — one per CPU core (multicore) Persistent Node.js SSR processes, per server worker ( = autosize to CPU cores, capped at 4) Directory with production artifacts Skip running build first Serve static assets directly (disable when a CDN hosts them) Path to an alternate config file Build + serve in one step By default, runs first. Skip this with : Multicore (worker processes) By default runs a single async server process, which uses one CPU core. To use every core on a multicore server, run one server worker process per core with (requires Pyxle 0.4.3+): Each worker is an independent server process with its own SSR worker pool, all sharing one listening socket — incoming connections are balanced across them with no load balancer and no shared state to configure. Throughput on CPUbound endpoints scales nearlinearly with the worker count. builds the project once before the workers start, so the build is never duplicated. Combine with only when already exists. Workers reconstruct their configuration from environment variables exported by the parent process — these are internal; don't set them yourself. SSR workers Serverside rendering runs in persistent Node.js processes that stay warm between requests: applies per server worker, so the total number of Node.js render processes is . For an SSRheavy app, is a good starting point — scale up only if profiling shows render queueing. Passing autosizes the pool to the machine's CPU count (capped at 4) per server worker. Sizing guidance Workload Suggestion APIheavy, little SSR SSRheavy pages , raise SSR workers if renders queue Small VPS (1–2 cores) Defaults () are fine Memoryconstrained Each worker is a full process — reduce before reducing Environment configuration Set production settings via environment variables: Or in a file: Variable Overrides Bind address Port number Debug mode (/ or /) Pages directory Static assets directory Build output directory CLI flags win over environment variables, which win over . Variables prefixed are exposed to client code — never put secrets in them. Reverse proxy setup In production, place Pyxle behind a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy, etc.) for TLS termination, load balancing, and static asset caching. Nginx example Caddy example CDN and edge caching Pages that render the same HTML for everyone a landing page, docs, marketing routes don't need to hit your origin on every request. Declare them in the [](../reference/configuration.md#edgecaching) block and Pyxle serves them (with a window) so a CDN or reverse proxy absorbs the load. This is what lets a small origin survive a traffic spike. Two things to know before relying on it: 1. A cacheable response carries no CSRF cookie, because a shared cache must never replay one user's token to another (and most CDNs won't cache a response that sets a cookie). Any reachable from a cached route must therefore be CSRFexempt hence the above. Only cache routes that render no peruser state and whose actions are safe to exempt. 2. The headers make a response eligible; the CDN still has to opt in. Many CDNs don't cache HTML by default: Cloudflare — add a Cache Rule (or Page Rule) with Cache Everything for the cached paths. Cloudflare honors for the edge TTL once the rule is in place. (Cloudflare ignores headers other than ; Pyxle's SPA navigation accounts for this and falls back to a normal fullpage load if the edge ever serves cached HTML to an inapp navigation request, so nothing breaks.) Nginx / Caddy / Varnish — enable (or the equivalent) for those routes; they respect out of the box. Contenthashed client bundles (under ) are already sent , and other static files get independent of the block above, which governs page responses. Docker Match to the container's CPU allocation — e.g. for a 4vCPU container. If you scale by running more singleworker containers behind a load balancer instead, keep the default. Health checks The scaffold includes a health endpoint at : Use this for load balancer health checks and monitoring. Process management (systemd) On a VM, run under a process supervisor so it restarts on failure and starts on boot: Build during deployment (), then — with the restart is fast because the unit only boots the server. Node.js must be on the service's for SSR. Checklist Before deploying: [ ] passes with no errors [ ] completes successfully [ ] Set in production [ ] Size to the machine's CPU cores (multicore serving) [ ] Configure CSRF if using HTTPS [ ] Add CORS origins if serving APIs to other domains [ ] Set up a reverse proxy for TLS [ ] Declare publiclycacheable routes in the block (and opt your CDN in) [ ] Configure health check monitoring on [ ] Add to Next steps Full CLI reference: CLI Commands Full config reference: Configuration

searchIndex.title: Editor setup
searchIndex.description: Pyxle Language Tools brings first-class IDE support to `.pyxl` files — syntax highlighting, diagnostics, completions, hover documentation, go-to-definition, and formatting. Everything works out of the
searchIndex.searchText: Editor setup Pyxle Language Tools brings firstclass IDE support to files — syntax highlighting, diagnostics, completions, hover documentation, gotodefinition, and formatting. Everything works out of the box with a single install. Beta release. The language toolkit is productionusable but still evolving. Report issues on GitHub. VS Code (recommended) 1. Install the extension Search for "Pyxle" in the VS Code Extensions panel, or install from the terminal: The extension is also available on the VS Code Marketplace. 2. Install the language server The VS Code extension is a thin client — the actual intelligence comes from the process. Install it alongside your project: Or install it as an optional extra with the framework: That's it. Open any file and you'll see the ✓ Pyxle indicator in the status bar when the language server connects. Features Syntax highlighting files get duallanguage highlighting — Python sections are highlighted as Python, JSX sections as JavaScript/React. The grammar correctly handles template literals, so code snippets inside backtick strings won't confuse the highlighter. Diagnostics Errors and warnings appear inline as you type: Python analysis — powered by pyflakes with 40+ rules. Catches undefined names, unused imports, syntax errors, unreachable code, and more. JSX analysis — powered by Babel. Catches syntax errors in your JSX code. Pyxlespecific rules — validates loaders (async, request parameter, return statement), functions, and component props, element format, and default export requirements. Completions Intelligent autocompletion for both sections: Python — powered by Jedi. Full module completions, function signatures, attribute access, import suggestions. JSX — powered by TypeScript's language service. React component props, DOM attributes, imported symbols, and local variables. Pyxlespecific — and decorator snippets, boilerplate, Pyxle component names (, , , , , , ), and property completions inferred from your loader's return statement. Hover documentation Hover over any symbol to see its type and documentation: Python symbols — function signatures, docstrings, and type info from Jedi. JSX symbols — type information from TypeScript. Pyxle decorators — hover or for a quick reference of the decorator contract. Pyxle components — hover , , , etc. for prop documentation. Gotodefinition Cmd+Click (macOS) or Ctrl+Click (Windows/Linux) to jump to definitions: Python symbols — jump to function definitions, class declarations, and imported modules via Jedi. JSX symbols — jump to component definitions, imported functions, and type declarations via TypeScript. Crosssection — click in JSX to jump to the key in your loader's return statement. Document symbols Open the Outline panel (Cmd+Shift+O) to see all symbols in the current file: loaders, actions, functions, classes, and JSX exports. Formatting Format files with sectionaware formatting: Python sections — formatted with ruff (or optionally black). JSX sections — formatted with prettier. Each section is formatted independently, preserving the file structure. Semantic highlighting Enhanced syntax coloring beyond the TextMate grammar — decorators, function parameters, builtin calls, and constants get distinct colors based on AST analysis. Configuration The extension exposes these settings (VS Code Settings → search "Pyxle"): Setting Default Description Command to launch the language server Arguments for the language server Python formatter (, , or ) JSX formatter ( or ) Enable pyflakes diagnostics Enable Babelbased JSX analysis Using a virtual environment If is installed in a virtual environment, point the extension to the full path: CLI tools The language toolkit also provides commandline tools for CI pipelines and scripting: Lint in CI Add to your CI pipeline to catch issues before merge: Other editors The language server speaks the standard Language Server Protocol and works with any LSPcompatible editor. Neovim Using nvimlspconfig: You'll also need to register the filetype: Sublime Text Using LSP: JetBrains IDEs JetBrains IDEs have limited LSP support. A dedicated plugin is planned for a future release. Troubleshooting "pyxlelangserver: command not found" The language server isn't on your PATH. Either: Install globally: Or set the full path in VS Code settings: No JSX completions JSX completions require Node.js and TypeScript installed in your project's . The language server spawns a TypeScript language service worker for JSX intelligence. No Python completions Python completions are powered by Jedi, which is installed automatically with . If completions are missing, verify the install: Status bar shows "✗ Pyxle" The language server failed to start. Check the Output panel in VS Code (View → Output → select "Pyxle Language Server") for error details. Formatting doesn't change anything Formatting requires the formatter binaries to be on your PATH: Python: (or ) JSX: (or install in your project) Architecture The language toolkit has a serverdriven architecture — all intelligence lives in the Python LSP server: Python analysis — Jedi runs inprocess for completions, hover, and gotodefinition. JSX analysis — An embedded TypeScript language service runs as a Node.js subprocess, communicating via NDJSON over stdin/stdout. Diagnostics — pyflakes for Python static analysis, Babel for JSX validation, plus Pyxlespecific rules. No virtual files — unlike some language tools that write temporary files to disk, Pyxle's language server keeps everything in memory. The VS Code extension is a thin LSP client (100 lines of TypeScript) that connects to the server and manages the status bar. All the intelligence is in the server, making it easy to support other editors. Source code: github.com/pyxledev/pyxlelangkit

searchIndex.title: Pyxle for AI coding agents
searchIndex.description: Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real full-stack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per
searchIndex.searchText: Pyxle for AI coding agents TL;DR. Pyxle is built so a coding agent — Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real fullstack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per page, Python backend, React frontend, zero config, structured errors, no magic. If your workflow involves pairing with an AI agent, Pyxle is the framework most optimised for that pairing. This isn't a marketing claim about being "AIpowered" — Pyxle doesn't call any LLMs itself. It's a claim about developer experience: the framework is shaped in a way that makes agents dramatically more effective per token spent. The problem agents have with modern web frameworks When a coding agent builds a feature in a typical Next.js + FastAPI stack, it has to simultaneously hold in mind: A TypeScript frontend and a Python backend — two languages, two type systems, two ecosystems, two build tools. A UI page component in . An API route in (or a separate FastAPI endpoint in a different repo). A datafetching hook in . A CORS config connecting the two backends. A shared types file (or worse, diverging types on each side). A , a , a , a , a , and usually at least one Dockerfile. For the agent, every one of those is context. Context is tokens, tokens are money, and every additional file the agent has to open is another opportunity to get the mental model wrong. Multifile edits are where most AI agents struggle the most — not because they can't reason about code, but because keeping N files consistent with each other across edits is genuinely hard. Now watch what the same feature looks like in Pyxle. One page, one file, both languages Here's the entire "list users and delete them" feature in Pyxle: That's it. One file, one route (), one feature. The agent reads this file, understands the whole thing in a single pass, and can make coherent edits to the loader, the mutation, and the UI without opening anything else. Compare the file count and crossfile coordination burden: What Next.js + FastAPI Pyxle Files for one feature 46 1 Languages TypeScript, Python Python, JSX Typeshare mechanism Manual / codegen Native dict Build configs 34 1 HTTP hops per render 12 (page → API) 0 (inproc) CORS to configure Yes No Context per feature High Low Why this matters for an AI agent specifically 1. Python is the language LLMs know best Every major LLM has seen dramatically more Python code during training than any other language. When an agent writes Python, it is operating in its highestconfidence mode. When it writes TypeScript, it is operating at one tier below that. When it writes the interaction between two languages — a TypeScript fetch call hitting a Python FastAPI endpoint — errors compound across the boundary and the agent has to reason about the contract in both directions simultaneously. Pyxle keeps the agent in Python for everything datarelated, and only asks it to write JSX for the view layer. The agent spends its strongest muscle on its strongest language. 2. Python is the language the AI ecosystem lives in Every serious LLM SDK — the Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK, LangChain, LlamaIndex, instructor, pydantic AI, the Claude Agent SDK — is Pythonfirst. Most have JavaScript ports that are perpetually behind in features. If your app integrates an LLM, a Pyxle loader can just directly. No microservice, no separate Python worker, no HTTP hop, no API token shuttling between frontends and backends. One file, one async call, response as a React prop. An agent building this feature doesn't need to open three repos. 3. No magic — the code you see is the code that runs Pyxle's and decorators set one attribute each and return the function unchanged. There is no dependency injection container, no runtime monkeypatching, no metaclass inheritance, no proxy object intercepting attribute access. You can read in a few seconds — it's around 150 lines including docstrings. This is a massive advantage for agents because the agent's mental model of the code matches the actual execution. In frameworks with heavy runtime reflection (Django admin, Spring, Rails, NestJS), the agent's debugging loop can break when its inferred behaviour doesn't match what the framework actually does at runtime. With Pyxle, the agent can reason locally. The decorator does nothing special. The function runs when the router calls it. The dict you return becomes the prop on the React side. That's all. 4. Conventional file layout is the routing table There is no , no , no route decorators. If you have , the route is . The agent doesn't have to open a router file to learn what routes exist — it can and be done. For an agent, this means zero tokens spent reading routing configuration. The filesystem is the configuration. 5. gives agents structured, perfile errors Most framework linters or type checkers print errors as a wall of text and the agent has to parse them. Pyxle's command prints diagnostics with a rigid, machinefriendly shape: Each line is followed by the file path on an indented next line. An agent can split on newlines, extract the file, the line number, and the message, and use that to plan its next edit. This is the format AI agents love. It's exactly what a grepbased tool call wants to consume. The checker also: Runs every file every time — one bad file never aborts the scan, so the agent gets a complete picture of project health after every edit. Reports both Python and JSX errors in the same pass. Suppresses cascade noise — fix Python first, and the downstream JSX errors that came from the broken Python vanish automatically. The agent isn't chasing ghosts. Has an exit code — if clean, if errors. Shellfriendly for agent workflows that gate subsequent commands on check success. 6. Tiny CLI surface The CLI is small and singlepurpose: (plus for TypeScriptchecking compiled JSX — that's the whole surface.) There is no , no , no . Every command has a clear, singlepurpose behaviour, and the agent doesn't have to search the docs to figure out which command does what. 7. Async by default, enforced at compile time AI SDKs are async. The Anthropic SDK, the OpenAI SDK, httpx, most modern database drivers — they all expect async. Pyxle enforces async at the parser level. is a compile error. is a compile error. The agent cannot accidentally write a blocking handler that would stall the event loop when calling an LLM. The framework protects against an entire class of mistake before the agent makes it. 8. The dev overlay shows agentfriendly errors in real time When a loader raises at request time, the dev overlay shows: The route that failed The exception type (e.g. ) The exception message verbatim A breadcrumb list describing which stage failed (loader, head, renderer, hydration) An agent with access to the browser (via a screenshot tool or a browser MCP) can read the overlay directly and understand the failure without needing to a log file. Most frameworks either print errors to the server log (invisible to the agent unless it knows to check), or render a generic 500 page with no useful detail. 9. The architecture is fully documented This is the part most frameworks don't bother with, and it's the part that matters most for an agent trying to make nontrivial changes. The architecture handbook is an 11document deepdive covering every subsystem of the framework: the parser, the compiler, the dev server, the SSR pipeline, the routing, the build, the runtime, and the CLI. It's written so that an agent can read one doc to answer one question, instead of inferring framework behaviour from source code. Specifically: The parser explains how files are split into Python and JSX. An agent debugging a parse issue can read this once and know exactly what's happening. Routing has a complete table mapping filenames to URLs (dynamic segments, catchalls, layouts, error boundaries). The runtime shows the full 83 lines of inline. There is no hidden decorator magi
headings: The problem agents have with modern web frameworks
One page, one file, both languages
Why this matters for an AI agent specifically
1. Python is the language LLMs know best
2. Python is the language the AI ecosystem lives in
3. No magic — the code you see is the code that runs
4. Conventional file layout is the routing table
5. pyxle check gives agents structured, per-file errors
7. Async by default, enforced at compile time
8. The dev overlay shows agent-friendly errors in real time
10. No codegen, no build artifacts the agent has to maintain
A concrete comparison: shipping a feature
Beyond individual features: what agents really want
The dev server reloads everything the right way
Error messages are written for humans *and* machines
The framework repo itself has a CLAUDE.md
Who should use Pyxle as their agent-first framework?
Python engineers shipping web apps with AI agents
Indie hackers using AI as their engineering team
Getting your agent set up with Pyxle
1. Drop a project-level CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) in your repo
2. Give the agent access to pyxle check and the dev server logs
3. Point the agent at the architecture handbook for deep questions

searchIndex.title: Migrating from .pyx to .pyxl
searchIndex.description: Pyxle's file extension has changed from `.pyx` to `.pyxl`. This guide explains why and how to update your project.
searchIndex.searchText: Migrating from to Pyxle's file extension has changed from to . This guide explains why and how to update your project. Why the change? The extension is already used by Cython, a wellestablished Python compiler. This collision caused real problems: GitHub misclassification — GitHub's linguist identifies files as Cython, causing repository language stats to show "Cython" instead of "Pyxle." IDE conflicts — VS Code, JetBrains, and other editors default to Cython syntax highlighting when opening files, requiring manual overrides. Search confusion — Searching for ".pyx files" returns Cython documentation and tutorials, making Pyxle harder to discover. Tooling friction — Linters, formatters, and CI tools with Cython support may interfere with Pyxle files. The new extension is unique, maps clearly to the "Pyxle" name, and eliminates all of these issues. What changed? Only the file extension. The file format, syntax, and behavior are completely identical: Before After Everything inside the files — Python server blocks, JSX client code, loaders, mutations — works exactly the same. How to migrate 1. Rename all files to 2. Update imports between pages If any of your files import from other pages, update the extension in the import path: 3. Update the VS Code extension If you're using the Pyxle VS Code extension (), update to the latest version. The new version registers as the file extension for syntax highlighting, diagnostics, and formatting. 4. Verify Run to confirm all files are recognized: You should see output like: Timeline This change was made before Pyxle's public launch, so no existing user projects are affected. If you cloned an early version of the framework or followed prerelease tutorials, this guide covers the onetime migration needed.

searchIndex.title: Configuration Reference
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is configured via `pyxle.config.json` in the project root. All fields are optional -- sensible defaults are used when omitted.
searchIndex.searchText: Configuration Reference Pyxle is configured via in the project root. All fields are optional sensible defaults are used when omitted. Full schema Directory settings Key Type Default Description Directory containing page routes and API files Directory for static assets served at Directory for compiled build artifacts Server settings Key Type Default Description Starlette server bind address Starlette server port (165535) Key Type Default Description Vite dev server bind address Vite dev server port (165535) Key Type Default Description Enable debug mode (hot reload, detailed errors) Middleware Applicationlevel middleware classes applied to every request. Each entry is a string pointing to a Starlettecompatible middleware class. Routelevel hooks applied to specific route types. Key Type Description Hooks for page routes Hooks for API routes Styling Key Type Description CSS files inlined on every page (relative to project root) JS files loaded on every page (relative to project root) CORS Key Type Default Description Allowed origins. CORS is disabled if empty. Allowed HTTP methods Allowed request headers Allow cookies and auth headers Preflight cache duration (seconds) CSRF Key Type Default Description Enable CSRF protection CSRF cookie name CSRF header name Set flag on cookie attribute (, , ) Paths exempt from CSRF checks (matched on segment boundaries) Exemption matching. An entry matches its exact path and anything beneath it at a segment boundary: exempts and , but not . Request paths are normalised first (/ resolved, repeated slashes collapsed), so is checked as . An entry of (or an empty string) is ignored rather than disabling CSRF sitewide — use to disable CSRF entirely. Shorthand to disable: Edge caching Mark page routes that render no peruser data as publicly cacheable. A matched page is served (plus a window) instead of the default , so a shared cache a CDN or reverse proxy can absorb the traffic instead of your origin. It's Pyxle's configdriven equivalent of Next.js perroute revalidation. Each key is a route pattern; each value is the sharedcache lifetime in seconds: Form Example Meaning Integer shorthand for the exact path Prefix wildcard for and anything beneath it Object form Same as the shorthand, with room to grow Matching. An exact path wins over a wildcard; among wildcards the longest (most specific) prefix wins. A pattern must be an absolute path. A wildcard () matches the bare prefix () and any segment beneath it (), but not a path that merely shares the string (). A cacheable response carries no CSRF cookie. A peruser must never ride on a response that a shared cache can replay to other users and most CDNs refuse to cache any response that sets a cookie at all. So on a cacheable route Pyxle omits the CSRF cookie, which means any reachable from that route must be exempt via [](#csrf). Only mark routes that render no peruser state and whose actions are safe to exempt. Key Type Default Description Map of route pattern → sharedcache lifetime. Empty = no shared caching (every page stays ). \ Lifetime in seconds (≥ 0) for the matched route. Turning it on at the edge. These headers make a response eligible for shared caching, but some CDNs don't cache HTML by default. On Cloudflare, you must also add a Cache Rule ("Cache Everything") for the cached paths the headers alone are necessary but not sufficient. See Deployment → CDN and edge caching. Navigation Pyxle's client keeps an inmemory navigation cache so clientside navigation and the data prefetched when a scrolls into view or is hovered resolves instantly instead of refetching the loader. The page you land on is seeded into this cache from the server render, so its loader never runs a second time on the initial load. Key Type Default Description Client navigation/prefetch cache settings. Lifetime in seconds (≥ 0) that a prefetched or seeded page stays fresh in the client navigation cache, for routes without a entry. disables navigation caching. A route listed in the [](#edgecaching) block reuses its edgecache TTL as its navigationcache lifetime, overriding for that route so a page's client freshness matches how long a CDN would serve it. (Consequence: raising a route's TTL also lengthens its edge cache, so purge the CDN on deploy which you should do regardless.) After a mutation, the client router's drops a cached entry so the next navigation refetches. Observability Request correlation IDs and timing. Both are on by default generating an id and reading two timestamps per request is submicrosecond and adds no I/O, and every request gets a correlation key surfaced as the response header and on (readable from any loader or ). See the Observability guide. Key Type Default Description \ Request observability settings. disables requestid and timing; enables both. Assign each request a correlation id, expose it on , and echo it as the response header. The request/response header carrying the correlation id. Honour a wellformed incoming id from instead of generating one. Leave off unless behind a trusted reverse proxy echoing a clientsupplied id is a loginjection/spoofing vector. Even when on, an incoming id must match or it is replaced. Measure wallclock request duration (to the response start). Expose a Prometheus metrics endpoint (request/render/loader/action durations and pagecache hit ratio). Off by default it exposes internal state. Path the metrics endpoint is served at when enabled. \ Optional bearer token: when set, the endpoint requires (compared in constant time). Emit one structured accesslog line per request (, , , , ). Off by default so it doesn't surprise existing log scrapers. (humanreadable) or (one JSON object per line). Accesslogger level (////). Emit OpenTelemetry spans for requests, SSR renders, loaders, and actions. Requires the extra; with it absent, enabling this fails loudly at startup. resource attribute for the tracer. Trace sampling ratio (0.0–1.0). Low by default so tracing can't swamp a busy server. The exporter endpoint comes from the standard env var. Shorthand to disable requestid and timing: . The pagecache hitratio metric requires the serverside page cache (active under ). Multiworker note: under each worker process exposes its own metrics (with a label), so aggregate across workers at the scraper. Plugins Each entry declares one plugin. Pyxle imports each plugin, calls its hook inside the ASGI lifespan, and exposes the services they register to loaders and actions. Entries can be either a bare string or an object: Key Type Default Description required Userfacing plugin name. Shown in error messages and used to namespace registered services. derived from Python module containing the plugin export. Default transforms → . Override for nonconventional package layouts. Attribute on that holds the class or instance. Pluginspecific configuration dict. The framework doesn't validate the shape — each plugin documents its own settings keys. Plugin ordering matters. Plugins are started in the order you list them. List a plugin before any plugin that depends on it — e.g. before , since auth needs a database. See the Plugins guide for authoring and consuming patterns, and Plugins API reference for the full API. Firstparty plugins: [](../plugins/pyxledb.md) — SQLitefirst database with migrations [](../plugins/pyxleauth.md) — Email+password session auth Environment variable overrides These environment variables override config file values: Variable Overrides Type string integer string integer , , / , , string string string Precedence From lowest to highest priority: 1. Pyxle defaults 2. 3. files 4. environment variables 5. CLI flags (, , etc.) Validation Pyxle validates your config file at startup: Unknown keys are rejected with an error listing the invalid keys Port numbers must be between 1 and 65535 Directory values must be nonempty strings Middleware entries must be nonempty strings CORS and CSRF subfields are typechecked Cache routes must be absolutepath patterns m

On a validation failure the action is not called and the client gets a with a map. See Validating request bodies for the full pattern and the [](cli.md#pyxleopenapi) command. Returns a JSON error response to the client. Autoimported since 0.3.0. Any file that declares at least one gets injected by the compiler, so works without you remembering to import it. A userdefined class (or an existing import) is respected and takes precedence. Constructor: Parameter Type Default Description (required) Error message sent to the client HTTP response status code Additional JSONserializable payload Perfield validation messages (field path → messages), surfaced to the client as . Keywordonly Properties: Property Type Description The error message HTTP status code Additional data (empty dict if None was passed) Perfield messages (empty dict if None was passed) A subclass of [](#actionerror) for requestbody validation failures. Defaults to HTTP and always carries . Pyxle raises it automatically when a Pydantictyped body fails validation; raise it yourself for checks Pydantic can't express (uniqueness, crossfield rules): Autoimported. Like , any file that declares at least one gets injected by the compiler. A userdefined class or existing import takes precedence. Constructor: Parameter Type Default Description Toplevel error message (required) Field path → list of messages. Keywordonly HTTP response status code Additional JSONserializable payload The client receives . The hook exposes the map as ; passes it as the second argument to . See Validating request bodies. Tell the client router to evict its cached navigation payloads for before the caller's next navigation. Used by handlers that mutate data the client has already cached elsewhere. The helper accepts either a plain dict (the usual return shape) or a Starlette / . In both cases Pyxle attaches an header to the outgoing response. and read this header automatically and call the clientside [](clientapi.md#invalidateurl) for each listed URL — so mutationdriven cache invalidation works endtoend with no extra wiring on the caller. Parameters: Parameter Type Description The action's return value. Modified in place and returned. URLs to invalidate. Empty / duplicate values are filtered. Returns the argument (possibly with a header or sentinel key added) so you can in one line. Background tasks — Fireandforget background work that runs inprocess on the app's event loop. See the Background Tasks guide. Schedule on the app's bounded worker pool and return immediately. Coroutine functions are awaited; plain callables run in a thread. Usable from any loader or action while the app is running. Raises When Called outside a running Pyxle app (no active queue) The bounded queue is at capacity (sustained overload) A task that raises is logged ( logger) and never takes a worker down. The queue is perprocess — under each worker has its own, and queued work is lost on restart. For durable or crossworker jobs, use a real job queue (Celery / ARQ / Dramatiq) — see the guide. Postresponse tasks in an Inside an , is a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/background/); runs after the response is sent. Returning from the action is shorthand for a single such task. WebSockets — A page that exports a modulescope also serves a WebSocket at its path. provides the pub/sub and auth helpers those handlers use. See the WebSockets guide for the full walkthrough. An async context manager that subscribes to a named channel for the life of the block (unsubscribing on exit, including disconnect) and yields a handle with : A published / is sent as a JSON frame, as text, as binary. The broker defaults to the appscoped on (one per process — see the guide's multiworker caveat). Implement the protocol ( / / ) for a Redis/NATS backend. Resolve the signedin [](../plugins/pyxleauth.md) user for a WebSocket upgrade, or . The auth middleware never runs for WebSocket scope, so a handler that needs the user must call this (it reads the session cookie off the handshake). Returns — zero database work — when the plugin isn't installed or no cookie is present. Whether the upgrade's is permitted. CSRF doesn't apply to a WebSocket, so checking the origin is the equivalent guard. An empty allows all; a missing header (sameorigin / nonbrowser) is allowed. Document elements Pyxle offers two ways to contribute elements to the document : the component (recommended) and the Python variable (lowerlevel alternative). Both are merged with automatic deduplication. component (recommended) Imported from , usable anywhere in your JSX tree: See Head Management for the full pattern (layouts, deduplication, reusable SEO components, etc.). variable (lowerlevel alternative) Not part of but available in every file. The variable is extracted at compile time by the parser; use it for fullystatic head metadata that doesn't need React context, or for pages without a JSX component. Static form: Dynamic form (callable): The callable receives the loader's return value. Must return a string or list of strings synchronously. For most dynamic head content, the component with normal JSX interpolation is simpler and more flexible. Explicit imports While the compiler autoinjects and , you can also import explicitly: This is useful for type checking and IDE support.

(default) — keep prefetched and seeded payloads fresh for 2 minutes. a large number — cache for the lifetime of the tab. searchIndex.title: Plugins API Reference
searchIndex.description: Every public symbol in `pyxle.plugins`, including the access helpers introduced alongside the plugin system.
searchIndex.searchText: Plugins API Reference Every public symbol in , including the access helpers introduced alongside the plugin system. Importing: For the userfacing guide, see Plugins. This page is the reference. Abstract base class plugins subclass to become discoverable. Nothing is abstract on the Python level — every hook has a noop default, so a minimal subclass is: Class attributes Attribute Type Default Description Userfacing plugin name. Used in error messages and for service namespacing. Informational. Pyxle doesn't enforce versioning today. Instance attributes Attribute Type Description Populated by the loader after construction. Matches the object from the entry in . Empty dict when the user didn't provide one. Methods Called once at ASGI lifespan startup. Typical work: open longlived connections, run migrations, register services. Raising here aborts ASGI boot (Starlette refuses to serve traffic), which is the right posture for "pyxledb can't reach the database" scenarios. Called once at graceful shutdown. Runs in reverse plugindeclaration order so earlier plugins' services are still available when later plugins tear down. Raising here is logged but doesn't abort shutdown of remaining plugins — besteffort by design. Returns middleware specs to append to the host app's stack. Each entry is : Default: empty tuple. See the Middleware contribution section of the guide for stackposition details. Perprocess lifecycle context shared across plugins. Plugins register named services here; loaders / actions retrieve them via or the modulelevel [](#pluginnamedefaultmissing) helper. Constructor is typically the host app's resolved . searchIndex.title: pyxle-db
searchIndex.description: `pyxle-db` is Pyxle's official database plugin over **SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL**. It offers two first-class paths: an **explicit-SQL** API (portable placeholders, a uniform `Row` type, checksum-t
searchIndex.searchText: pyxledb is Pyxle's official database plugin over SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL. It offers two firstclass paths: an explicitSQL API (portable placeholders, a uniform type, checksumtracked migrations — you write SQL, it makes that SQL portable) and an optional SQLAlchemy ORM path. Either way, every loader and action gets a requestscoped database handle, and writes commit or roll back automatically. Version 0.3.0. SQLite needs nothing beyond the package; PostgreSQL, MySQL, and the SQLAlchemy ORM ship as extras (the base install stays SQLAlchemyfree). Every backendspecific behaviour documented here is enforced by test suites that run against real PostgreSQL 16 and MySQL 8 servers in CI. Install The extra includes because MySQL 8's default authentication requires it at connect time. Quickstart Add the plugin to (see the plugins guide for how plugin loading works): At startup the plugin opens the database, applies any pending migrations, and registers the shared instance. Loaders and actions reach it through : Plugin settings All settings are optional — with none, you get SQLite at . Key Default What it does SQLite file path, relative to the project root. — Full database URL (takes precedence over ). Supports indirection. Directory of migration files, applied at startup. Milliseconds to wait for a SQLite file to appear before failing (useful when another process creates it). Never commit credentials. The setting supports indirection — the committed config names an environment variable, the deploy environment supplies the secret: Startup fails with a clear error if the named variable is unset, rather than silently falling back to SQLite. The plugin registers three services: (the ), (the connection URL with credentials redacted), and — for SQLite — (the resolved file path). Database URLs A bare filesystem path is also accepted and treated as SQLite. Server backends accept pool sizing as URL options: Other PostgreSQL options (such as ) pass through to asyncpg as server settings. The MySQL backend pins every pooled session to UTC (), so columns and are never shifted through the server's system time zone. Queries and rows Four query methods, identical on every backend: returns the affected row count. Every read returns the same type — immutable, accessible by index and by column name: is the bridge to whatever model layer you prefer; no integration code needed: Placeholders Always write . pyxledb rewrites it to each backend's native style — for SQLite, / for PostgreSQL, for MySQL — so the SQL in your codebase never forks per engine. When you need a literal question mark (PostgreSQL's JSON operators), escape it as : The rewriter is literalaware: inside string literals, quoted identifiers, comments, and dollarquoted bodies is never touched, so data can't become SQL structure during translation. Transactions The transaction object carries the full query surface (, , , , ). Two escape hatches are SQLiteonly and raise on server backends: and — prefer , which works everywhere. Requestscoped access and autotransactions With the plugin installed, every loader and action gets a lazy database handle on — no import, no service lookup. A request that never queries opens no connection. On an unsafe method (///) the request's writes run inside one transaction that commits when the action succeeds and rolls back when it fails — where "fails" means the action raised (or any exception), which Pyxle turns into a non2xx response. You never call /, and a failed action never leaves a partial write behind. / run readonly. Opt out per action with (then manage yourself), or appwide with . The ORM path (SQLAlchemy) Prefer an ORM? Install and set in the plugin settings. Models subclass ; loaders and actions get a requestscoped on under the same autotransaction rules: SQLAlchemy errors surface as the same pyxledb error types as the explicitSQL path. The base install stays SQLAlchemyfree. The CLI The CLI reads the same + the app uses. Pick one migration tool per app: the checksum migrator for explicitSQL, Alembic for the ORM. Datetimes One contract on all three engines, in both directions: Reads always return timezoneaware UTC objects — including from naive columns, which are interpreted as UTC. Binds accept either naive datetimes (assumed UTC) or aware ones (converted to UTC before binding). Without this, the engines disagree sharply: asyncpg rejects aware datetimes for columns outright, and MySQL's driver would silently serialise an aware datetime's foreign wall clock. pyxledb normalises at the backend boundary so application code never thinks about it. Migrations Migrations are plain SQL files in the configured directory, named : Rules the migrator enforces: Applied exactly once, atomically. Each migration's statements and its trackingtable insert commit together or not at all. Checksumtracked. Editing an alreadyapplied migration is detected and rejected — write a new migration instead. Perdialect overrides. replaces the base file on that backend (the example above uses MySQLspecific DDL while SQLite and PostgreSQL share the base file). An override with no base file is a backendonly migration. Namespaced tracking. The default tracking table is ; libraries that bring their own migrations onto your database (pyxleauth does) use a private tracking table via so two migration histories never see each other as drift. The plugin applies pending migrations at startup. You can also drive the directly: Writing portable schemas These rules are proven against real PostgreSQL and MySQL servers — the live conformance suites enforce them: for every key or indexed column. MySQL cannot index bare (error 1170). SQLite and PostgreSQL treat exactly like , so nothing is lost. Keep for payloads. columns are fine on SQLite and PostgreSQL. On MySQL prefer via a perdialect override: MySQL's is capped at 2038 and rounds to whole seconds. MySQL has no . Create indexes in migrations (they run exactly once) or probe first. Spell out inserted values instead of relying on column s, which drift subtly between engines. Errors One hierarchy regardless of driver — application code never imports , , or : Exception Raised when Base class for everything below. Constraint violation (unique, foreign key, …). Connection refused, pool exhaustion, server gone. Bad URL, bad pool option, unset variable. SQLiteonly call on a server backend. found no row. / Malformed or edited migrations. The DatabaseLike contract Plugins that need a database (like pyxleauth) don't depend on the class — they bind to the protocol: , , , an asynccontextmanager , and a property. Any object satisfying it, registered as the service, can stand in — an adapter over another engine, a test fake. is the reference implementation, and the protocol is : The full replacement contract (error translation, dialect names, datetime semantics) is documented in the protocol's docstring and exercised by pyxleauth's contract test suite. Using it without the plugin Scripts, tests, and workers can open a database directly: , , , and expose the backend name, parsed configuration, SQLite file path, and a perinstance query counter (handy in tests). See also Plugins guide — how plugin loading, ordering, and services work. Configuration reference — the schema. pyxleauth — the official auth plugin, built on this contract.

searchIndex.title: pyxle-auth
searchIndex.description: `pyxle-auth` is Pyxle's official authentication plugin: email + password accounts, sliding sessions, password-reset and email-verification flows, role-based access control, and scoped API tokens — wir
searchIndex.searchText: pyxleauth is Pyxle's official authentication plugin: email + password accounts, sliding sessions, passwordreset and emailverification flows, rolebased access control, and scoped API tokens — wired into your app with one config entry. It never sends email and never renders UI; it gives you hardened primitives and stays out of your templates. Version 0.2.0. Runs on pyxledb (SQLite, PostgreSQL, or MySQL — the full account lifecycle is tested against real servers in CI), or on any database layer satisfying the contract. Install This pulls in and . There are no other dependencies. Quickstart List before in — the auth services run on the database that plugin opens: That's the whole wireup. At startup the plugin applies its bundled migrations (idempotent, checksumtracked, in its own tracking table so they never collide with your app's migrations) and registers its services. Protect a page with a guard in its loader: Signin must put a header on the response, so it lives in an API route — actions return plain JSON payloads and can't attach cookies: has the same shape and also returns . returns a cookie that clears the browser's copy — set it the same way. Plugin services Service name Type What it does Accounts, sessions, password flows. Roles and permissions. Singleuse tokens (resets, invites, magic links). Longlived bearer tokens. The resolved configuration. and are typed shortcuts for the first and last. How sessions work The browser holds 32 random bytes (256bit entropy) in an cookie — default name . The database stores only the SHA256 hash of that value, so a leaked database cannot resurrect sessions. Sessions are sliding: each resolved request can extend the expiry (), up to an absolute maximum age. Defaults: 30day sliding window, 90day absolute cap. signs out everywhere; / power a "manage devices" page. Password changes and resets revoke every session automatically. Session middleware, , and Listing the plugin installs automatically. On every request it resolves the session cookie and sets (a , or when anonymous) — so loaders and actions can read the signedin user without calling a guard. A request without the cookie does zero database work; one with it does a single indexed lookup that the guards then reuse, so a guarded loader never resolves the session twice. The middleware also serves the endpoints the client [](../reference/clientapi.md#useauth) hook talks to, under (default ): Endpoint Purpose The current user as JSON. Sign in with . (optout) Create an account with . (optout) Revoke the session and clear the cookie. and reuse / — same rate limiting, same enumerationsafe errors — and map failures to status codes ( invalid credentials, account exists, weak password, unverified email, rate limited with ). They are statechanging POSTs, so the framework's CSRF protection applies; sends the token for you. Set to turn them off and drive signin from your own instead (then call ); and stay available. The signedin user is seeded into the server render (), so shows the right state on the first frame — no flash of "logged out", no extra roundtrip. Guards Dropin checks for loaders, actions, and API routes: Guard Behaviour or — never raises. , or raises for loaders. , or raises for handlers. User must hold the permission, else 401/403. Same, for actions. Extracts a token from the header, or . The roadmapnamed aliases / and / are the same functions as / — call them at the top of a loader or action (Pyxle guards are awaited, not wrapping decorators): Passwords Hashing is argon2id via . Defaults: time cost 3, memory 64 MiB, parallelism 2. In strict mode (the default) the settings refuse to construct with parameters below the floor (time cost ≥ 2, memory ≥ 19 MiB), so a typo'd config can't silently weaken hashing. Two deliberate guards worth knowing about: Passwords longer than (default 1024) are rejected before hashing — unbounded input would make argon2 itself a denialofservice vector. Signin burns the same argon2 verification cost whether or not the account exists, so response timing doesn't leak which emails are registered. Password reset and email verification pyxleauth never sends email. Flows that need delivery return a raw, singleuse token exactly once; your app puts it in a link and hands it to whatever mailer it already uses: The user completes the flow with , which burns the token and revokes every session. Email verification mirrors the pattern: returns a token, redeems it. Both raise for anything stale, used, unknown, or wrongpurpose — indistinguishably. Tokens are stored hashed (SHA256), are singleuse, and requesting again invalidates earlier tokens for the same purpose. The unknownemail path of performs the same committed token write a real account does, so even its timing doesn't enumerate accounts. For your own flows (invite links, magic links), the same machinery is exposed as : , , . Roles and permissions A small RBAC layer with wildcard grants: Permissions are dotseparated strings; a role can hold exact permissions, wildcards, or the global . Also available: , , , , and (the user's full effective set). Granting twice is idempotent. API tokens Longlived bearer credentials for CLIs and CI, prefixed so secret scanners can recognise them: returns the token metadata only if the token exists, is not revoked or expired, and carries the required scope — otherwise , with no reason disclosed. , , and complete the lifecycle. When is exceeded, raises ; the countandinsert is racesafe on PostgreSQL and MySQL. In an API route, pair it with the guard helper: Rate limiting Signin, signup, and passwordreset are ratelimited per identifier (email and, when provided, IP) with hourly buckets stored in the database — no Redis required. Limits are settings (defaults: 10 signins, 5 signups, 3 resets per hour). One deliberate subtlety: a correct password is never blocked by the peremail bucket — only failed attempts count toward it — so an attacker hammering an inbox can't lock the real owner out of signing in. Settings Configure in (camelCase), override per environment with variables. Precedence: config environment default. Config key Env variable Default (30 d) (90 d) unset (30 min) (24 h) Strict mode is the production posture and the default: it requires and enforces the argon2 strength floors, refusing to boot otherwise. Local HTTP development relaxes it through the environment — never in the committed config: Bring your own database pyxleauth binds to the plugin service, not to the pyxledb package. Any plugin that registers an object satisfying [](pyxledb.md) can back it — an adapter over another engine, a test fake. The replacement must also translate uniqueconstraint violations into (that's how duplicate signups become ) and report a the DDL helpers understand. The package's runs the entire lifecycle against a deliberately foreign database object — it is both the executable specification and a template for writing an adapter. Errors All inherit from : Exception Meaning Wrong email/password — message intentionally vague. Duplicate signup. Below (or absurdly long). Bucket exhausted; carries . Signin blocked while is on. Reset/verify token stale, used, unknown, or wrongpurpose. APItoken cap hit. Granting an undefined role. OAuth signin (Google, GitHub, Discord) Social signin ships in the subpackage behind the extra (). Enable it with the setting: Client credentials are read from the environment only — never put them in : A signin link is just an anchor to the start endpoint: The middleware redirects to the provider, handles the callback, creates or links the local account, sets the session cookie, and sends the user to . On failure it redirects to with (, , , , ). Security model — the callback is a carrying an attackerinfluenceable , so the defenses are deliberate: PKCE is mandatory; the verifier lives only in the signed cookie. A signed, singleuse, HttpOnly cookie binds the flow to the browser; the the provider echoes must equal the cookie's nonce (constanttime) —
