searchIndex.description: `pyxle-mail` is Pyxle's official email plugin: one `mail.service` your loaders, actions, and API routes call as `await mail.send(...)`, delivered over SMTP, [Resend](https://resend.com), or any backen
searchIndex.searchText: pyxlemail is Pyxle's official email plugin: one your loaders, actions, and API routes call as , delivered over SMTP, Resend, or any backend satisfying the contract. You write the send once and choose the provider by config, not code. With no configuration it logs the email instead of sending it, so local development works with zero setup. Version 0.1.0. SMTP and the console provider need nothing beyond the package; Resend ships as an extra. It depends on no other plugin — no database, no auth — so its position in the list doesn't matter. Install Quickstart Add the plugin to (see the plugins guide for how plugin loading works): That's the whole wireup. At startup the plugin builds a provider from your settings and registers the . With no configuration it registers the console provider — it logs a summary and sends nothing, so there's no account, key, or SMTP server to stand up for local work. Send from any loader, action, or API route through : Configure a provider (below) when you want real delivery. Plugin services The plugin registers two services: Service name Type What it does The consumerfacing surface — builds, validates, and delivers messages. The resolved configuration. is the typed shortcut for the first; it raises if isn't in your list. Providers A provider is what actually puts a message on the wire. The service wraps one provider and is what your app calls; swapping providers is a config change, never a code change. Provider When Needs Local dev, dryrun — logs a summary, sends nothing. The default. nothing Any mail server (Gmail, Fastmail, MailHog, a corporate relay). host (+ usually user/password) Resend — modern transactional API. + API key forces the console provider regardless of — the safe switch for staging or a first deploy, where you want the wiring exercised but no mail to actually leave. Example: Resend in production Resend requires a verified sender domain (SPF/DKIM DNS records) before it will deliver from anything but its test address. Sending mail The sender ( / ) and fall back to the configured defaults, so most calls pass only , , and a body. Recipients are validated and normalised before they reach the provider. raises for a bad message — no recipient, no body, an empty subject, or a malformed address — before any network call, and if the provider rejects the message or the transport fails. Both inherit . When you'd rather build the message yourself, is the lowerlevel entry point; it applies the default sender the same way. The MailProvider contract 's swappable piece is the provider — the analogue of pyxledb's . Any object satisfying can back the service: the bundled SMTP/Resend/console providers, or a community adapter for SendGrid, Mailgun, SES, Postmark, and so on. The protocol has two members: The protocol is , so verifies the members are present (signatures are checked statically): and are both frozen and provideragnostic, so the same message roundtrips through any provider unchanged. Settings Configure in (camelCase), override per environment with variables. Precedence: config environment default. Keep secrets (SMTP password, Resend API key) in the environment, never in the committed config. Config key Env variable Default What it does — Default sender. Required for any real provider. — Optional display name. — Default . \ \ . Force the console provider regardless of . — SMTP server host. SMTP port. — SMTP auth user. — SMTP auth password (env only). STARTTLS (port 587). Implicit TLS (port 465). — Resend API key (env only). A misconfigured real provider fails loud at startup, not on the first send: with no key, with no host, or any real provider with no refuses to boot. Unknown settings keys are rejected too, so a typo can't silently take the default. Errors All inherit from : Exception Raised when Base class for everything below. Unknown provider, or a real provider missing its key/host/fromaddress. No recipient, no body, empty subject, or a malformed address. The provider rejected the message, or the transport failed. What pyxlemail is not Honest scope, so you can plan around it: Not a newsletter / campaign system. It sends transactional and oneoff mail. Audiences, scheduling, and analytics are out of scope. Not a template engine. Pass rendered HTML/text — use Pyxle components, Jinja, or fstrings to produce it. Not a queue. A awaits the provider. For high volume or retries, enqueue with a backgroundjobs plugin and send from the worker. See also pyxleauth — its passwordreset and emailverification flows hand you a token for your mailer; this is that mailer. Plugin standards — the bar every official plugin meets. Plugins guide — how plugin loading, ordering, and services work. API routes — where cookiesetting endpoints live. The plugins directory — every official and community plugin. </content </invoke

searchIndex.title: Plugin Standards
searchIndex.description: The bar a plugin must clear to be listed in the [pyxle.dev plugin directory](/plugins). It exists so that anything a Pyxle user installs from the directory behaves the way the official plugins do — pr
searchIndex.searchText: Plugin Standards The bar a plugin must clear to be listed in the pyxle.dev plugin directory. It exists so that anything a Pyxle user installs from the directory behaves the way the official plugins do — predictable lifecycle, honest docs, failsecure defaults. The official packages (pyxledb, pyxleauth) are held to every rule on this page; read their source when a rule's intent is unclear, it is the reference implementation of all of them. Community plugins are listed under their author's name and are not securityaudited by the Pyxle team. The directory label says exactly that. Naming The bare namespace is the official namespace. We don't preregister or squat names — an official plugin is published when it actually ships, with real code behind it. The namespace is protected by PyPI's namedispute process (PEP 541): if someone uploads a confusing or malicious package, we dispute it on brand grounds, not by parking empty packages ahead of time. Community plugins ship under your own PyPI name — , , anything honest. The directory lists you under the name you chose; the name has no bearing on review. A foundingcohort plugin that clears the bar may be granted a canonical at the team's discretion — fulfilled by publishing your (real, working) plugin under that name, not by us holding it empty for you. Whatever you pick, don't imply officialness (, will be asked to rename). One plugin, one job. "Auth plus storage plus cron" is three plugins. Packaging Everything on this list ships in the wheel — a user who runs and never visits your repo still gets all of it: with real metadata: description, license, classifiers, , project URLs. Depend on with an honest floor (the version whose APIs you actually use). file (OSIapproved) inside the package directory, not just the repo root. marker and full type hints on the public API. with: a configsnippet quickstart a stranger can follow in two minutes, a settings table (key, default, what it does), and a scopehonesty section — what the plugin deliberately does not do. The official plugins' "What it is not (yet)" sections exist because unstated scope becomes angry issues. (Keep a Changelog format) and semantic versioning. Pre1.0 is fine — say so. Optional heavy dependencies go in extras (), never in the base install. Behaviour Subclass and do real work in — open clients, validate settings, register services. Fail loud at startup: a misconfigured plugin should refuse to boot with an actionable message, not limp into requests. Register services under your namespace: , — and document every name you register. Consumers reach them via . Never monkeypatch Pyxle internals. Use the public plugin API only. If the API can't do what you need, that's an issue (or an RFC comment) — not a patch. Don't depend on sibling plugins unless the dependency is the point and is documented — the way depends on . When you need a database, build against [](pyxledb.md), not the concrete class, and say so. Settings come from the plugin's block in ; secrets come from the environment (support indirection or read env directly). A committed config file must never need to contain a credential. If your plugin owns database tables, ship checksumtracked migrations with a namespaced tracking table () so they coexist with the host app's migrations. Testing A real test suite, runnable with from the package directory, green in CI on every supported Python version. Test the plugin lifecycle, not just the library: startup with valid/invalid settings, service registration, shutdown. If you claim support for an external system (a database, an email provider, a queue), back the claim with a conformance test that runs against the real thing in CI — service containers are cheap. A claim no test enforces will eventually be false. Security Failsecure defaults. The lazy path must be the safe path; relaxation is explicit and documented (the way pyxleauth requires to weaken cookies for local dev). Credentials and tokens are hashed or encrypted at rest; raw values are shown once and never logged. Document your attack surface: what the plugin exposes, what it trusts, what the operator must protect. Provide a security contact in your README and respond to reports. Directorylisted plugins with an unpatched, reported vulnerability are delisted until fixed. Maintenance A directory listing is a small ongoing promise: Keep CI green against the latest Pyxle minor (we announce breaking changes in the changelog; pre1.0 minors may include them). Respond to security reports within a reasonable window. If you're done maintaining it, archive the repo and tell us — an honestlyarchived plugin is respectable; a silently dead one gets delisted. The review Submissions (see the directory page for how) get a review against exactly this page — nothing unwritten. We check the packaging list mechanically, read the source for the behaviour and security sections, and run the suite. Outcome is a listing, a short list of blockers, or (rarely) a "this should be an app pattern, not a plugin." Reviews are public; expect honest notes.

searchIndex.title: Plugin Ideas
searchIndex.description: Capabilities the ecosystem needs, scoped to what the plugin API supports **today** — services and middleware (see [the plugins guide](../guides/plugins.md); page contribution is [Phase B, in RFC](rfc-
searchIndex.searchText: Plugin Ideas Capabilities the ecosystem needs, scoped to what the plugin API supports today — services and middleware (see the plugins guide; page contribution is Phase B, in RFC). Every idea below is buildable right now, and each names the contract it builds against. If you take one on, read the standards first and check the directory so two people don't race the same idea blind. The first idea here shipped. Mail — once "the most wanted one" on this list — is now the official pyxlemail plugin (transactional email over SMTP, Resend, or the console). This list is where official plugins start. Sizes are honest estimates for a working, standardsmeeting v0.1: S ≈ a weekend, M ≈ a week of evenings, L ≈ a real project. On names: these are capabilities, not package names to grab — ship under your own PyPI name ( is the official namespace, and a foundingcohort plugin may later be granted a canonical name by publishing under it). The identifiers below are the suggested API a plugin registers, not its package. Storage (M) File storage: with , S3/R2/GCS behind extras, localfilesystem backend for dev (mirroring how pyxledb treats SQLite as the zeroconfig default). Builds against: the service registry; indirection for credentials. Caching (S) A TTL cache service: plus a helper for loader functions. Inmemory backend by default, Redis behind an extra. Builds against: the service registry. Observability (S) Sentry (or OpenTelemetry) wiring: middleware that captures unhandled exceptions with request context, an service for manual events, scrubbing rules for secrets. Small surface, huge production value. Builds against: plugin contribution + the service registry. Rate limiting (S) Reusable rate limiting: a middleware for pathpattern limits and a service for inaction checks. Databasebacked buckets via (no Redis required), Redis behind an extra. pyxleauth ships exactly this pattern internally — generalise it. Builds against: [](pyxledb.md), middleware contribution. Feature flags (S) Feature flags on a table: , percentage rollouts, an friendly API. Pairs naturally with pyxleauth's . Builds against: , optionally pyxleauth's user model. Payments (M) Stripe (or similar): for checkout/portal sessions and a webhookverification helper (constanttime HMAC, timestamp skew, idempotencykey tracking via ) that apps call from their own API route. Webhook handling stays in the app; the hard, easytogetwrong crypto lives in the plugin. Builds against: the service registry, for idempotency. Search (M) Meilisearch/Typesense client as a service: index management, a API shaped for loaders, reindex helpers. The docssite search on pyxle.dev is handrolled today — this is the generalisation. Builds against: the service registry. Background jobs (L) A backed queue: , a worker entrypoint (), retries with backoff, deadletter table. The hardest and most valuable item on this list — talk to us before starting; it's a strong Phase B codesign seat. Builds against: (transactions, portable schema rules). OAuth / social login (L) OAuth/OIDC signin helpers: provider services (, ) implementing the code flow, state/PKCE handling, and a documented recipe for the appside API routes that exchange the code and call to establish the session. Fills pyxleauth's explicitlyoutofscope gap without forking it. UI pages stay in the app until Phase B. Builds against: the service registry, pyxleauth's session API. Building one? Open a submission issue early (see the directory) so it's visible — and so we can offer review while you build rather than after.

searchIndex.title: RFC: Plugin Routes & Pages (Phase B)
searchIndex.description: Phase A (shipped, stable) lets a plugin run lifecycle hooks, register named services, and contribute ASGI **middleware** — see the [plugins guide](../guides/plugins.md). What it cannot do is own a URL
searchIndex.searchText: RFC: Plugin Routes & Pages (Phase B) Status: draft, open for comment. This is the design discussion for the next phase of the plugin system — plugins contributing routes and, eventually, whole pages. Nothing here is shipped; signatures are sketches. Comment by opening an issue on pyxledev/pyxle titled . Authors in the Founding Plugin Program codesign this — their build experience against today's API is the primary input. Where the line is today Phase A (shipped, stable) lets a plugin run lifecycle hooks, register named services, and contribute ASGI middleware — see the plugins guide. What it cannot do is own a URL: no API endpoints, no pages. Every route in a Pyxle app comes from the app's own tree. That line is why pyxleauth's quickstart still asks you to write yourself, and why an adminpanel plugin — the Djangoadmin moment this framework should eventually have — can't exist yet. Phase B1 — plugin API routes (proposed, concrete) The smaller, earlier slice: plugins contribute API endpoints (Starlettestyle handlers — no compilation, no client bundling). Proposed rules, each open to challenge: Namespacing by default. Plugin routes mount under (). A host app can remount via config; collisions between two plugins are a startup error, not a silent override. Host wins. If the app's tree defines the same path, the app's endpoint is used and a startup warning names the shadowed plugin route. CSRF is declared, not assumed. Each route declares its posture: (default — framework CSRF applies), (Origin/Referer check for preauth endpoints that can't have a token yet), or (signatureverified webhooks). The config's mechanism stays for app routes; plugin routes carry their posture with them so installing a plugin can't silently widen the app's CSRF holes. Introspectable. (CLI) lists every route with its origin — app file or plugin name — because debuggability is where mountedroute systems usually rot. What B1 unlocks immediately: pyxleauth ships its own signin/signup/signout/reset endpoints and its quickstart shrinks to config plus a ; webhookreceiving plugins (Stripe) own their endpoint with the right CSRF posture out of the box. Phase B2 — plugin pages (design space, deliberately unresolved) Plugins shipping real pages — signin screens, admin panels. The hard questions, with current leanings: 1. Compilation. Hostbuild compiles pluginshipped sources (leaning: yes — plugins ship sources, the host's discovers and compiles them; precompiled artifacts would freeze plugins to compiler versions). 2. Bundling. Plugin client code joins the host's Vite graph — one dedup'd React, plugin chunks hashed like app chunks. This is the riskiest area (version skew, CSS collisions) and needs a prototype before promises. 3. Mounting & precedence. Same model as B1: prefixmounted by default (), host remounts via config, host pages shadow plugin pages with a warning. 4. Layouts & theming. Plugin pages render inside the host's root layout by default (they should look like your app, not the plugin author's). Styling contract — tokens/CSS variables vs. utility classes — is an open question; this is where foundingauthor input matters most. 5. Security. A plugin page runs the plugin's loaders/actions in your process; B2 doesn't change the trust model (installing a plugin always meant running its code at startup), but routeowning plugins make the audit surface more visible. The directory's review bar (see standards) tightens for pageshipping plugins. Capability contracts (the bigger principle) Routes and pages are the how; this is the why. [](pyxledb.md) didn't just ship a database — it shipped , a protocol pyxleauth and any other plugin build against, so the concrete database is swappable. That pattern is the ecosystem's real differentiator, and the intent is to generalise it: frameworkdefined (or thincontractpackage) interfaces for the capabilities the ideas list keeps circling — — so an app writes and swaps the provider by config, not code. Pyxle ships the interface; the community ships the adapters. The directory then organises by capability with one recommended provider and alternatives behind it, instead of by package name. This is design intent, not a shipped API; the contracts land alongside the plugins that need them, and is the template for all of them. Sequencing B1 is targeted for an upcoming minor; it's mostly registry plumbing plus the CSRFposture mechanism. B2 lands after the founding cohort's feedback and a bundling prototype — a wrong API here would be very expensive to walk back, and we'd rather be slow than stuck. Capability contracts are incremental — each interface ships with its first real consumer, the way already did. Open questions (comment on these) 1. Should B1 routes be able to declare middlewarestyle guards (auth required) declaratively, or is "call the guard in the handler" enough? 2. Route remounting config: perplugin prefix, perroute overrides, or both? 3. For B2, do plugin pages get access to host services by name only (), or a declareddependency mechanism with startup validation? 4. Is "host page shadows plugin page" ever the wrong default? (e.g. a securitycritical plugin page an app shouldn't be able to accidentally replace.)
headings: Phase B1 — plugin API routes (proposed, concrete)
Phase B2 — plugin pages (design space, deliberately unresolved)

searchIndex.description: Welcome. This section is the **architecture handbook** — a guided tour of how Pyxle is built on the inside, written for everyone from "I just installed Pyxle yesterday" to "I want to send a PR that to
searchIndex.searchText: Pyxle Architecture Welcome. This section is the architecture handbook — a guided tour of how Pyxle is built on the inside, written for everyone from "I just installed Pyxle yesterday" to "I want to send a PR that touches the SSR worker pool." If the rest of the docs answer what does Pyxle do?, this section answers how does it do it, and why was it built that way? You don't need to read these in order. Pick whichever subsystem you're curious about and start there. Each page is selfcontained, links liberally back to the source code, and uses real examples from real files. A 30second mental model A Pyxle application is, fundamentally, a directory of files. Each file is one page. It contains Python (your loaders, actions, and head metadata) and JSX (your React component) — colocated in a single file because they describe the same thing: one route. When you run , six pieces of code spring into action: That's the whole story. Every doc in this section is a closer look at one of those six pieces. The handbook Start here Overview — The full request lifecycle from URL to HTML, end to end, in one read. If you only read one doc here, read this one. The core compiler The .pyxl file format — Why Pyxle invented a new file extension and what's actually in one. The "two languages, one file" idea. The parser — How Pyxle splits Python from JSX without any fence markers, using only . Includes the multisection walker, the brokenPython detector, and the tolerantmode diagnostic system. This is the most sensitive code in the framework — and the most fun. The compiler — How parsed pages become + + artifacts on disk, including the JSX import rewriter and the runtime injection pass. The serving stack Routing — Filebased routing, dynamic segments (), catchall routes (), optional catchalls (), route groups (), and collapsing. The dev server — Starlette + Vite + the file watcher + the incremental builder + the WebSocket error overlay. The whole experience explained. Serverside rendering — How a request becomes HTML. Loader execution, the Node.js worker pool, head merging from four sources, document assembly, streaming responses, and clientside navigation. Production Build and serve — What actually does, how the page manifest is constructed, and how runs a built app without Vite in the loop. Crosscutting The runtime — The and decorators and the zeromagic contract that lets your code remain pure Python. The CLI — , , , , . Config precedence, tolerantmode validation, and how the CLI bridges user input to the rest of the framework. Conventions used in this section Source citations look like — the file is relative to , and the number is the line where the thing starts. Click through; the docs are written so you can read them with the source open in a second window. "Pyxle in [language X]" boxes compare a Pyxle decision to how Next.js, Django, FastAPI, or Remix would handle the same thing. They're optional context for readers coming from other frameworks. Examples are real, runnable, copypasteready code — never pseudocode. Every snippet either is, or could be, a real file. "How it works under the hood" sections drop into the actual algorithms and data structures. These are the "advanced" parts — feel free to skim or skip if you're new. Design principles Reading the architecture is easier if you know the rules the framework was built to follow. There are seven, and they're enforced by the test suite: 1. Pythonfirst, not Pythononly. Great Python and great React. Neither half is a secondclass citizen. 2. Convention over configuration. Zero config for the common cases. and you're running. 3. Compilerdriven. Metadata is extracted at build time, not runtime. The parser does the heavy lifting once, so the request path stays fast. 4. No magic. Decorators add metadata, they don't wrap or transform. is a oneline decorator that sets a single attribute. You can read it. You can call it. You can debug it. There is no DI container, no metaclass, no runtime patching. 5. Progressive disclosure. Simple things are simple. Complex things are possible. You shouldn't need to know about the SSR worker pool to ship your first page — but if you want to, every layer is documented and accessible. 6. Batteries includable. Pyxle ships hooks and integration points, not opinions. Bring your own ORM, your own auth, your own state management. 7. AIfirst DX. Predictable patterns, strong types, clear errors. Frictionless for both humans and coding agents. You'll see these principles cited throughout the architecture docs. When a design choice looks surprising, it's almost always one of these seven that's driving it. A note about depth These docs are deliberately deep. They're written with the assumption that you want to understand Pyxle well enough to: Debug a confusing error message by tracing it back to the line of code that emitted it. Confidently file a bug report with a minimal reproduction. Send a pull request that fits the existing architecture. Build your own framework that learns from Pyxle's choices. If you just want to ship a feature, the Core Concepts and Guides sections are friendlier starting points. Come back here when you're curious. Welcome aboard. Let's take a tour. → Start with Overview.

searchIndex.description: This is the one doc to read first. We're going to follow a single HTTP request all the way from `localhost:8000/` to your browser, naming every component it touches and every transformation it goes th
searchIndex.searchText: Overview — A request, end to end This is the one doc to read first. We're going to follow a single HTTP request all the way from to your browser, naming every component it touches and every transformation it goes through. By the end of this page you'll have a working mental model of the entire framework. To make it concrete, we'll trace the homepage of a fresh project — the one with a Pyxle logo and a "You're ready to build with Pyxle" heading. The starting point: one file Here's the file we're going to render, simplified slightly: That's the whole file. Notice three things: 1. There's no separator between the Python and the JSX. Pyxle figures out where one ends and the other begins by parsing the file with Python's module — the parser is the most interesting subsystem in the framework, and it gets its own deepdive doc. 2. The decorators are tags, not wrappers. doesn't transform — it just sets on the function so the framework can find it later. You can read the decorator source in ten seconds: it's three lines. See The runtime. 3. is the recommended way to control the document head. During SSR the compiler extracts the children of and merges them with head contributions from layouts and parent components. Pyxle also supports a legacy Python variable that the parser picks up at compile time, kept around for backward compatibility, but is the idiomatic choice for every real page. See SSR § Stage 3: resolving the head. Stage 0 — starts Before any request can arrive, the dev server has to be running. When you type , this is what happens: 1. Load configuration. The CLI looks for , parses it into a frozen dataclass, applies environmentvariable overrides (), then applies CLI flag overrides (, , etc.). Precedence is CLI env file default. Source: . Details: The CLI. 2. Build the initial page set. The compiler walks , parses every file, and writes three artifacts per file into : — the Python loader, executable — the React component, bundleable — extracted route info Details: The compiler. 3. Start Vite. Pyxle spawns Vite as a subprocess on port 5173, pointed at the justgenerated . Vite handles JS/CSS bundling, hot module replacement, and React Refresh. Pyxle's dev server proxies asset requests to Vite — Vite never sees the user's HTTP requests directly. Details: The dev server § Vite integration. 4. Start an SSR worker pool. By default, Pyxle starts one persistent Node.js worker that stays alive for the life of the dev server. The worker speaks newlinedelimited JSON over stdin/stdout. Each render roundtrip is 3080ms; spawning a fresh subprocess per request would be 200400ms. Details: SSR § Worker pool mode. 5. Start the file watcher. Pyxle watches , , and any global stylesheets/scripts. When a file changes, the watcher debounces for 250ms (so saving twice in quick succession is one rebuild), then recompiles only the changed files. Details: The dev server § The watcher. 6. Start Starlette on port 8000. This is the ASGI app that actually answers your browser. The router has separate branches for pages, API routes, action routes (), client assets, public assets, and a catchall 404 handler. Details: The dev server § The Starlette app. When all six are up, the console shows: You're ready to take requests. Stage 1 — The browser asks for You open in Chrome. The browser sends: Starlette's router receives this and dispatches to the handler that registered for the route — that handler is a closure created by (). The handler resolves which file owns this route by looking it up in the page registry (). The registry was built during the initial compile — it maps each route path to a dataclass containing every path the SSR pipeline needs: That , plus the request, plus the dev server settings, get passed to in . This is the SSR entry point. Everything that follows lives inside it. Stage 2 — Run the loader Pyxle imports the compiled server module (). In dev mode, before importing, it purges any cached version from so changes you've made to the file are reflected immediately. (In production, modules are imported once at startup.) Once imported, Pyxle finds the function tagged — that's — and calls it with the Starlette : A few invariants matter here: Loaders are always async. The parser refuses to compile a sync . This is enforced at compile time so you can never accidentally block the event loop. Loaders take exactly one positional argument named . The parser checks this in and emits a structured error if you violate it. Loaders return a JSONserializable dict. If you return something that can't be serialized, the framework raises and shows a friendly overlay (in dev) or a generic 500 (in prod). If the loader raises , Pyxle walks up the directory tree from the current page looking for the nearest file. If one exists, it renders that boundary with the error context. If not, it falls back to the default error document. Details: SSR § Error handling. Stage 3 — Resolve the head While the loader's data is the body of the page, the is assembled from up to four sources, in order of increasing priority: 1. Layout blocks. If (or any ancestor layout) has a JSX block, its contents go in first. 2. The page's Python variable. Static or callable. If it's a callable, Pyxle invokes it with the loader data: . 3. The page's JSX blocks. If the React component renders a block, that contributes too. 4. Runtime registrations (rare; used by some advanced helpers). () deduplicates these by tag identity: — only the highestpriority one wins. — deduped by . — deduped by . — singleton. — deduped by . Anything with — deduped manually. Tags without a clear identity (e.g., a second preconnect) are kept. Each element is also sanitized: event handler attributes (, ) are stripped, and inside are escaped, and / URLs are neutralized. For our , the merged head ends up as: Plus whatever Vite injects (the HMR client tag, the React Refresh preamble) and Pyxle's own boilerplate (charset, viewport). Stage 4 — Render the React component Pyxle now needs to render to a string of HTML. This happens in (). In worker pool mode (the default since v0.1.7), the renderer sends a JSON message to the next available SSR worker: Inside the worker (a longrunning Node.js process running ), each render does: 1. Bundle the component with esbuild. All imports get inlined into a single JS string. esbuild is fast — typically under 30ms per file even for nontrivial component trees. Cached across requests for the same file. 2. Evaluate the bundle in a fresh context. The default export is the function. 3. Render with . This produces a plain HTML string for the component tree, including any JSX blocks (which the worker also returns separately so they can be hoisted into the document head). 4. Reply with another newlinedelimited JSON message containing . The worker pool is just roundrobin over N workers, with automatic respawn if a worker crashes. See SSR § Worker pool mode. What comes back from is a dataclass with the body HTML, any extracted head elements, and any inline blocks the component injected. Stage 5 — Assemble the document in glues everything together into a complete HTML document. It produces a shell — a and of strings — so the body HTML can be slotted in for streaming. Here's the rough structure of the shell: There are a few subtle things going on: The props are inlined as JSON inside a tag. When React hydrates on the client, it reads this script's text content, parses it, and uses it as the initial props. JSON inside a script tag is safe from XSS as long as is escaped to — which Pyxle does in . A CSP nonce is generated for every response. It's a 24byte URLsafe random token, attached to all tags Pyxle emits. If you set a strict ContentSecurityPolicy header, the nonce flows through automatically. The shell is split into prefix + suffix so the response can be streamed: prefix → body HTML → suffix. The browser starts parsing the before the loader's data has even been serialized. Stage 6 — Stream the response Pyxle wraps everything

searchIndex.title: The .pyxl file format
searchIndex.description: A Pyxle page is a single file with two languages in it: Python and JSX. This doc explains *why* that file exists, *what's actually in it*, and
searchIndex.searchText: The file format A Pyxle page is a single file with two languages in it: Python and JSX. This doc explains why that file exists, what's actually in it, and what mental model the rest of the framework uses for it. If you want to know how Pyxle splits the two languages, that's the next doc — The parser. Why one file? Web pages are the unit of work for most web developers. "I'm working on the checkout page. I'm fixing the 404. I'm adding a settings page." The page is the noun. In most frameworks, that one mental noun is spread across at least two files: a backend handler in one place and a UI component in another. That separation has merits — different languages, different runtimes, different deployment surfaces — but it also has a real cost. Every nontrivial change has to keep the two files in sync. Pyxle's bet is that colocating both halves of a page in one file is worth it, even at the cost of inventing a new file extension. When you change the loader, the component is right there. When you rename a prop, both sides update in the same diff. When you delete a page, you delete one file. This isn't a unique idea — Vue's files, Svelte's files, Astro's files, MDX's files all do something similar. What's different about is that the two languages inside are the full ones: real Python (with , , , decorators, asyncio, the works) and real JSX (with , hooks, ES module syntax, the works). Neither is a strippeddown DSL. What a file looks like Let's start with the simplest one and grow it. A pureJSX page This is valid. Some pages just don't need a loader. Pyxle is fine with that — the parser sees zero Python content, the compiled file is empty, the route is registered but has no function. Pyxle in Next.js terms: This is the equivalent of a Next.js page that doesn't export . It's a static component with no serverside logic. A page with a server loader The Python comes first, then the JSX, in that order. The two halves are separated only by whitespace and the natural transition from Python statements to ES module syntax. The parser will figure it out. A few things to notice in this example: is a decorator with no parentheses. It's not a factory function or a wrapper — it's a pure tag. See The runtime. The loader is async. Always. The parser refuses to compile a sync one. The loader takes a single argument named . This is also enforced by the parser. The return value is a plain dict. It will be serialized to JSON and passed to the React component as a prop named . The JSX block looks like a regular React file. , an , JSX syntax. There's nothing Pyxlespecific in the JSX section. You could copypaste it into a standalone file and it would work. A multisection page Here's a more interesting pattern: alternating Python and JSX blocks. This one has four alternating sections: Python helper → JSX helper → Python loader+action → JSX export. The parser handles arbitrary alternation. There's no fence marker, no separator comment, no special syntax. The boundaries are detected by parsing — see The parser. Aside: Pyxle used to require explicit and markers to split sections. We removed them in v0.1.7 because the ASTdriven walker is reliable enough that markers became noise. Old files with markers still parse correctly because the markers are valid Python comments — they just no longer mean anything. A page with document metadata Use the component from to control what ends up in the document head. It's the recommended approach for nearly every page: Pyxle's JSX compiler recognises blocks during parsing, extracts them as metadata, and during SSR the head merger hoists them into the document alongside contributions from parent layouts. Dynamic content interpolates through normal JSX expressions (), and nested components can contribute their own head elements — see SSR § Stage 3: resolving the head. For the rare case where a page has no JSX component at all and still needs head metadata, Pyxle also supports a compiletime Python variable: The parser extracts it from the AST at compile time and stores it in the page metadata. It supports strings, lists of strings, or a callable that receives the loader's return value. For most real pages, reach for instead — the JSX form is easier to write, easier to typecheck, and composes naturally with layouts. What's not in a file A file is always one route — one page. It is not: A library file. If you have shared Python utilities, put them in a regular file and them from your . The parser does not look inside files. A reusable component file. If you have shared JSX components, put them in a regular file and them from your . The bundler resolves these like any normal module import. An API handler. Plain JSON APIs go in files (regular Python, no JSX). See Routing § API routes. A layout or template. Layouts use the same extension but live in files at strategic points in the directory tree. They wrap pages instead of being pages themselves. See Routing § Layouts. What the framework "sees" When the compiler processes your file, it produces a single object that captures everything the rest of the framework needs. The shape is: Source: . A few things worth understanding here: 1. and are strings, not ASTs. The parser does parse them with to find segment boundaries and extract metadata, but the output is just two strings ready to be written to disk as the compiled and files. 2. and are line maps. When the compiler finds an error in the joined Python output (say, line 7 of ), it can look up to find the original line number in the source file. This is how Pyxle's error messages always point at the right line. 3. , , , , , and are extracted at parse time. The dev server doesn't have to reparse anything to answer questions like "does this page have a loader?" or "what tags should be injected before hydration?". The metadata is precomputed once and read at request time. 4. is populated only in tolerant mode. When runs the parser, it passes . Errors that would normally raise are collected as entries instead, so a single check pass can report every error in every file at once. File extension and tooling Pyxle uses because: It signals "this isn't a normal Python file" to anyone glancing at the directory. Most editors don't have Pyxle support yet (LSP support is on the roadmap), so the convention is: set your editor to treat as Python for syntax highlighting. The Python half lights up correctly; the JSX half ends up looking like a string of nonsense, which is honest about the situation. The Cython project uses for a different purpose. We know. Pyxle is for web pages, Cython is for compiled Python extensions — in practice they don't overlap, and the Pyxle parser has nothing to do with the Cython parser. When the Pyxle LSP ships (separate package, ), editors will get correct duallanguage highlighting, jumptodefinition for both halves, and inline diagnostics. Until then, the convention is "highlight as Python and squint at the JSX". What if I'm coming from another framework? You know... And you're wondering... Answer Next.js Where's ? It's the decorator on a function called whatever you want. Next.js Where's and ? One file: the loader and the component live together in . Remix Where's and ? for loaders, for actions. Same idea, different name. Django Where's ? The loader function is the view. The template is the JSX in the same file. FastAPI Where's the route decorator? Pyxle uses filebased routing — no . The file path is the route. Rails Where's the controller? The loader is the controller. The action is the controller's mutation handler. Flask Where's ? Same as FastAPI: filebased. SvelteKit Where's ? One file, not two. The Python half is . The recurring theme: Pyxle compresses what other frameworks split into multiple files into a single file per route, while keeping the two halves in their original languages. Where to read next You now know what a file is, what's in it, and what the parser produces from it. The next question is: how does the parser actually split Py

What if I'm coming from another framework?

searchIndex.description: The honest answer is: by **parsing**. The Pyxle parser feeds the source to Python's `ast.parse` and uses the structure of the result — and the structure of the failure when parsing fails — to find the
searchIndex.searchText: The parser "How does Pyxle know where the Python ends and the JSX begins?" The honest answer is: by parsing. The Pyxle parser feeds the source to Python's and uses the structure of the result — and the structure of the failure when parsing fails — to find the boundary between the two languages. There are no fence markers, no comment directives, no perline heuristics, no whitespace conventions. There is only a walker, a greedy strategy, and a small handful of carefully chosen guardrails. This doc is the deepest technical doc in the architecture section. It's written like a tour: we'll start with the problem, build up the algorithm one step at a time, then catalog the edge cases that the algorithm has to handle. By the end you'll be able to reason about any file the parser will see in the wild — and you'll know exactly why each guardrail exists. File: (1100 lines, the most sensitive code in the framework). The problem in one sentence Given a string that contains some Python and some JSX, find the boundaries between them, extract metadata about the Python half, and emit clear errors when the file is broken. Sounds simple. Let's see why it isn't. Why the obvious approaches don't work You might reasonably ask: "Why not just look for ? That's a JSXonly line." This is the first thing every framework that does this kind of split tries. It works for the easy 95% and breaks on the hard 5%. Let's see why. Approach 1: keyword matching This was Pyxle's original approach (prev0.1.7). It's intuitive: Python lines start with Python keywords. But it fails on: is valid in both languages. You cannot tell from the line alone which one it is. You need context. It also fails on multiline constructs like dictionary literals or template strings: A pure linebased scanner would have to track open brackets, open strings, indentation — and at that point you've reinvented half of a Python parser, and it's still wrong. Approach 2: explicit fence markers Pyxle supported this for a while. It works perfectly, but it has two problems: 1. It's annoying. Every file needs the marker comment, and forgetting it is a frustrating "why won't this compile?" error. 2. It's fragile. A comment inside a JS template literal (i.e., a string that mentions the marker) would be misread as a real fence. We had this exact bug in our own playground page — a codedisplay widget that quoted the marker as part of an example. The marker scanner would split the file in the wrong place. Approach 3: parse it This is the approach Pyxle uses now. The insight is: Python's is the ultimate ground truth for "is this Python?". If says yes, it is. If it says no, it isn't. No heuristics needed. Once you accept this, the algorithm almost writes itself. The greedy walker Here's the core idea, in plain English: Start at line 0. Try to parse the whole rest of the file as Python. If it parses, you're done — the whole file is Python. If it fails, the parser tells you exactly which line it failed on. Walk back from that line until you find the largest valid Python prefix. Mark that prefix as a Python segment. Then look at where Python stopped: that line and onward is JSX. Walk forward through the JSX until you find a line where Python parses again. Mark that span as a JSX segment. Repeat. Concretely: Source: . That's the entire walker. Eight lines of orchestration around two helpers. The helpers do the interesting work. This function answers: "Starting at this line, how far can Python take me?" The naive version would be: walk forward one line at a time, parse each prefix, stop when one fails. That's O(n²) in the worst case. The clever version uses CPython's own information: when fails on a multiline input, the attribute usually points exactly at the line where things first went wrong. We can jump straight there. Source: . The walkback is bounded by the SyntaxError lineno, so in practice this finishes in 12 attempts, not n. The worst case is when the parser emits a misleading lineno (it sometimes does — Python's error recovery isn't perfect), but even then we walk back one line at a time and terminate at the empty prefix. Notice that we aren't doing anything fancy with the AST itself. We don't traverse it, we don't extract symbols, we just ask "does this string parse?" and use the answer as a yes/no oracle. The AST extraction comes later, after the segments are concatenated. Why try the whole suffix first? Because the most common case is "this file is mostly Python with a JSX section at the bottom". For that file, the optimistic parse on the first call is the only parse needed. The walker terminates after one invocation and we move on. Most real files take fewer than 10 milliseconds to parse endtoend. This function answers: "I'm in a JSX section starting at this line. Where does it end?" Simple version: walk forward one line at a time, calling at each line, returning the first line where Python becomes possible again. That works most of the time, but it has a sneaky bug — the bug that sent us looking for a smarter approach. The bug Consider this JSX: The lines and are inside a JS template literal. They look like Python. They even parse as Python. But they aren't Python — they're string content. A naive would walk forward, hit the line, ask , see "yes, this parses as Python", and incorrectly split the JSX function in half. The fix walks the JSX with structural awareness. searchIndex.title: The compiler
searchIndex.description: The compiler is the bridge between the parser and the rest of the framework. The parser produces an in-memory `PyxParseResult`; the compiler turns that result into **three files on disk** that the dev
searchIndex.searchText: The compiler The compiler is the bridge between the parser and the rest of the framework. The parser produces an inmemory ; the compiler turns that result into three files on disk that the dev server, the SSR pipeline, and the Vite bundler all consume: This doc walks through what's in each of those files, why they exist as separate artifacts, and the small but important transformations the compiler applies along the way. Files: (70 lines) — toplevel entry (310 lines) — and the runtimeimport injection helpers (370 lines) — the import rewriter (125 lines) — Babel subprocess wrapper (130 lines) — , , and the small declaration dataclasses The toplevel entry: Source: . This is the only function the rest of the framework calls. Its job is small: 1. Resolve the pagerelative path (the file's path inside ). 2. Compute the route paths (primary and any aliases — see Routing). 3. Parse the source file with . 4. Hand the result to to emit the three files. 5. Return a describing what was written. It's deliberately thin. All the interesting decisions live in the parser and the writer. Three artifacts, three jobs 1. The Python artifact () is the executable Python module for one route. It's what and actually and call. Here's a real example. Source : Compiled : A few things happened: The JSX is gone. The compiled is purely the Python half of the source. The dev server never imports the JSX. was added at the top. The source file uses without importing it because the user never has to import Pyxle's runtime decorators — the compiler does it for them. Same for . The original imports () are preserved verbatim. The compiler doesn't reformat your code, doesn't reorder imports, doesn't rewrite anything. The only change is the runtime import insertion. 2. The JSX artifact () is the bundleable JSX module for the same route. Vite reads it, bundles it with esbuild, and ships it to the browser. For our example: This is a verbatim copy of the JSX half of the source — almost. The "almost" is the JSX import rewriter (next section). 3. The metadata artifact () is the extracted metadata for the route. It's a small JSON document the dev server reads at startup to build its routing table without reparsing the source: The dev server's () loads all of these at startup, builds a , and uses it to dispatch requests. No file is parsed during a request — that work is done once, at compile time. The runtime import injection pass When you write: …you don't write first. Pyxle's philosophy is that the framework should stay out of your way for the common case. The compiler adds the import for you, but in a careful, ASTaware way. The injection logic lives in three helper functions in : — adds — adds — adds the combined import when both decorators are present The compiler chooses one of the three based on what the parser found: Source: . Why ASTaware? A naive version would do: This works for most files. But it breaks on: PEP 257 says the module docstring must be the first statement. PEP 236 says must come before any other code (after the docstring). A naive prepend would put the runtime import before the docstring, breaking both PEPs and producing a on the next compile cycle. The injection helper uses to find the right insertion point: after the docstring, after any imports, but before any other code. It also checks for an existing and skips the injection if one is already present (so you can import it explicitly if you want). The complete logic is short — about 70 lines of careful AST walking in . Pyxle in plain Python: This is the only place where the compiler "modifies" your code. Everywhere else, the compiled file is byteforbyte the same as the Python half of your source. The injection is the smallest possible change consistent with not requiring you to write boilerplate imports. The JSX import rewriter JSX files import each other. A page might import a shared component: But after compilation, doesn't exist on disk anymore — its compiled version is . The bundler can't resolve because there's nothing there. We need to rewrite the import specifier so it points at the compiled artifact: The rewriter () handles this. It's a characterbycharacter JS lexer that walks the JSX source tracking: When we're inside a string literal (, , ` `/// ... /fromimport(...)import x from "..."import "..."export ... from "..."import("...").pyxl?query#fragment.jsxcompiler/jsximports.py:1372import\s+\w+\s+from\s+"'["']const msg = "import was removed"// import './foo.pyxl'import("./" + name + ".pyxl")export { foo } from "./bar.pyxl"<Script src="..." strategy="..." /<Image src="..." width="..." height="..." /<Head...</Head<headjsxcomponentextractor.mjscompiler/jsxparser.py:33127Resultcomponentserrorvalidatejsx=Truenpm install.pyxlcompiler/core.pycompiler/writers.py:28136.pyxlcompilefile()compilefile()@serverexport defaultpages/api/.pycompiler/writers.py:1415PageMetadatacompiler/model.py:66.jsonPageMetadatadevserver/builder.py.pyexport default function ....jsx.pyxl.pyxl.pyxlpyxle build` takes the same compiled artifacts and packages them for production deployment, including how it bridges Pyxle's compiled JSX to Vite's bundle output via the page manifest.

searchIndex.title: Routing
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses **file-based routing**: the path of a `.pyxl` file inside your `pages/` directory *is* its URL. There is no `@app.route("/")` decorator. There is no router config file. There is no list of
searchIndex.searchText: Routing Pyxle uses filebased routing: the path of a file inside your directory is its URL. There is no decorator. There is no router config file. There is no list of routes maintained by hand. The directory structure is the routing table. This doc explains how that works, exhaustively. By the end you'll know exactly which URL each file maps to, what the dynamic segment syntax means, how layouts compose, and how a request gets dispatched to the right handler. Files: (135 lines) — file path → URL conversion (zero dependencies, importable from anywhere) (280 lines) — , , , dataclasses (380 lines) — loads metadata, builds the route table at startup The basic mapping The simplest case is one file per URL: Three rules turn the file path into the URL: 1. Strip the extension. 2. Drop segments — collapses to its parent. 3. Prefix with — every route starts at the root. A nested directory becomes a path segment: That's the entire convention for static routes. There's nothing clever happening — is the function that does it, and it's about 40 lines of straightforward string handling. Pyxle in Next.js terms: This is exactly how Next.js's directory works. The conventions are deliberately compatible because filebased routing is one of those design choices that's hard to improve on. Dynamic segments — Real apps need URLs that contain parameters. Pyxle uses square bracket syntax in filenames to declare them: The brackets become a Starlette path parameter. Inside the loader, you read it from : The parameter name is whatever you put inside the brackets: You can have multiple dynamic segments at different levels: Dynamic segments can sit alongside static ones: Routing is mostspecificfirst, so will match rather than the dynamic . (This is Starlette's standard behaviour — Pyxle doesn't override it.) Parameter name sanitization The parameter name inside brackets is sanitized for Starlette: hyphens, dots, and special characters get replaced with underscores. A name starting with a digit is prefixed with . Empty brackets fall back to (or for catchalls). Source: . In practice this is invisible — name your parameters with regular identifiers and you'll never notice. The sanitizer exists to make sure that even unusual filenames don't break route registration. Catchall segments — Sometimes a single route should match an arbitrary number of path segments. For example, a docs site where the URL should be served by one component that reads the path as a list. Pyxle uses for this: The converter tells Starlette to match everything (including slashes). Inside the loader, is a single string like . You can split it on to get the segments. Catchall routes only match URLs with at least one segment after the prefix. So matches and but not itself. If you want to match as well as , you need the optional catchall (next section), or you can add an sibling: Optional catchall — The doublebracket form matches everything including the empty case: This is the same as a catchall plus an sibling, but in one file. It's useful when you have a single component that should render for the entire URL space under a prefix — for example a storefront that handles , , and all with the same loader and component. Under the hood, Pyxle creates two Starlette routes for an optional catchall: a primary route and an alias. They're both served by the same handler. The returned by has both: Source: . Route groups — Sometimes you want to organize files in a folder without the folder becoming part of the URL. Pyxle uses parentheses for this: The and segments are invisible to routing but visible to layouts. This lets you apply different layouts to different parts of the app — say, a marketing layout for the public pages and an app shell for the authenticated section — without forcing the routes themselves to be prefixed with or . You can nest route groups inside dynamic segments and vice versa: Source: (just — six lines). A complete reference table Here's every routing feature in one table: Source filename Route Notes Index of root Static route Index collapses Dynamic segment Nested under dynamic Catchall (=1 segment) <br Optional catchall (primary + alias) Group is invisible Group is invisible API route (Python only) (not a route) Layout — wraps siblings (not a route) Error boundary (not a route) 404 boundary The last three are special filenames — they're not routes themselves; they affect how other routes render. We'll cover them in the next sections. Layouts A file at any level of the tree wraps every route below it. For example: The dashboard's route is rendered as: Layouts compose from outermost to innermost. The compiler generates a route module in that does the nesting: The page metadata's field points at this composed module instead of the raw page file, so the dev server and the bundler load the wrapped version automatically. The layout discovery + composition logic lives in . When you add or remove a , the file watcher detects the change and rebuilds every route under that directory's subtree. A looks like a regular page, but its component receives a prop instead of : The layout can have its own loader — it runs in addition to the page's loader, and its data is available to the layout component via the prop. (The page component still gets its own .) Templates: layouts that don't persist A is like a but with one key difference: templates remount on every navigation. A layout keeps its DOM and React state across navigation; a template throws everything away and rebuilds. This matters for things like animations that should restart on every page change, or analytics that should fire once per page view. Use for "stateless wrapper around the page" and for "persistent shell that survives navigation." Error boundaries A file at any level catches errors raised by descendants. When a loader raises , or when a component throws during SSR, Pyxle walks up the tree from the current route looking for the nearest and renders it. If 's loader raises, Pyxle first tries . If that also fails (or doesn't exist), it falls back to . If neither exists, it falls back to the framework's default error document. The error boundary component receives the error context as a prop: In dev mode, the error boundary also receives the Python traceback in for debugging. In production this is omitted — production responses must not leak internal state. The boundary discovery logic lives in . Notfound boundaries A file is the same idea, scoped to 404 responses. When a request hits Pyxle and no route matches, the framework walks up from the request path looking for the nearest : A request for first tries . A request for falls through to . If neither exists, the framework returns a plain 404 response. API routes Pages are JSXrendered HTML. API routes are pure JSON endpoints under . They use plain files (not ) because they have no client component: An API route module exports an async function with a name matching the HTTP method: The dev server inspects the module at registration time, finds the , , etc. functions, and registers a Starlette endpoint for each. There's no decorator — the function name is the contract. If a single function should handle multiple methods, name it instead and Pyxle routes all methods to it: API routes can use the same dynamic segment syntax as pages (, , etc.). Action routes decorated functions inside a page get their own endpoint at . This is invisible — you never write the URL — but it's how the clientside hook talks to the server. For example, given: …the dev server registers: → page handler (loader + component render) → action handler (POST only) → action handler (POST only) The clientside hook returns a function that POSTs to the URL with whatever payload you pass it. The action returns its result as JSON. Action names must be unique within a page (the parser enforces this). Across pages, names can collide — but each file registers its actions independently, so the routing is perpage. Action routing lives in . How a r

searchIndex.title: The dev server
searchIndex.description: `pyxle dev` is the command you'll spend the most time with. It runs a Starlette ASGI app on port 8000, a Vite subprocess on port 5173, an SSR worker pool, an incremental compiler, a file watcher, and
searchIndex.searchText: The dev server is the command you'll spend the most time with. It runs a Starlette ASGI app on port 8000, a Vite subprocess on port 5173, an SSR worker pool, an incremental compiler, a file watcher, and a WebSocket error overlay — all coordinated as a single async program. This doc explains how those pieces fit together. By the end you'll understand what every line in the startup banner means, what happens when you save a file, and how to read the dev server's source code. Files (): File What it does (290) The class and the toplevel lifecycle (820) Creates the Starlette ASGI app and routers (150) The frozen config object (100) Walks and computes file content hashes (165) Orchestrates one incremental build pass (350) Watches the filesystem and debounces events (370) Spawns and supervises the Vite subprocess (155) Forwards Viteserved URLs to Vite's port (380) Loads compiled metadata into a (280) The / / dataclasses (295) Generates layoutwrapped client modules (105) WebSocket overlay for error notifications (140) Discovers and boundaries (225) Perroute middleware policies (75) Loads custom user middleware modules (300) Optional Tailwind CSS watcher (160) CSRF protection middleware (2170) Bundled client runtime sources , Global script and stylesheet resolution That's a lot. Most of it doesn't matter for understanding how the dev server works at a high level. The key pieces are: the Starlette app, the builder, the watcher, Vite, and the registry. Everything else is supporting infrastructure around those five. Lifecycle in one diagram When all seven steps are done, the console shows: You can read those lines in order against the seven steps above — each log is one piece of the lifecycle reporting that it's done. The Starlette app () is the factory function that builds the entire ASGI application. It returns a instance with: Routes Page routes (, line 291) — one Starlette per in the route table. Each route has a closure handler that knows which page to render. API routes (, line 187) — one Starlette endpoint per file. Functions named after HTTP methods (, , etc.) get registered for those methods; a function named gets all methods. Action routes (, line 363) — POSTonly endpoints under for every decorated function. Static asset mount (, line 498) — serves and directly from disk. Public files mount (, line 485) — serves whatever's in . Health endpoints — and for orchestration. Catchall 404 — walks up the request path looking for the nearest boundary. WebSocket route at — used by the dev overlay client. Middleware stack Listed from outermost to innermost (outermost runs first on request, last on response): 1. GZip — production only 2. CORS — if is configured in 3. CSRF — if is true in config 4. — shortcircuits requests for and public assets so they don't reach the page router 5. Custom user middleware — anything declared in 6. Vite proxy — dev only, forwards JS/CSS/HMR requests to Vite The middleware stack is built in line 668. Each middleware is added with and Starlette chains them in order. Lifespan hooks Starlette has a callback that runs on startup and shutdown. Pyxle uses it to: Start the SSR worker pool on startup Stop the worker pool gracefully on shutdown (give workers 5 seconds to exit cleanly, then kill any holdouts) This means workers are alive for the lifetime of the dev server, not perrequest. The incremental builder () is the function that runs one build pass — initial compile, or rebuild after a file change. It: 1. Scans with to find every , , and client asset file. For each file, it computes the SHA256 hash of the contents. 2. Compares hashes against the previous build's metadata to find which files actually changed since last time. 3. Compiles only the changed files by calling for each. Unchanged files are left alone. 4. Copies API modules () to their build location. 5. Composes layouts for any pages whose ancestor files have changed (). 6. Syncs global stylesheets and scripts declared in the config. 7. Removes orphaned artifacts for source files that have been deleted since the last build. 8. Returns a dataclass with counts: pages compiled, APIs copied, etc. The hashbased diffing is the key to performance. A typical 50page project has thousands of unchanged files at any moment; running the parser on every one of them on every save would be wasteful. With hash diffing, a singlefile edit triggers exactly one call. Source: . When does a layoutonly change trigger a page rebuild? Layouts are tricky: when you edit , every page under needs its composed route module regenerated. The composed module is what bundles the layout with the page, so it needs to be reemitted whenever the layout's identity changes. The builder handles this by recompiling layouts first, then rerunning the layout composition pass for any page whose ancestor layout was rebuilt. This is invisible to you — you save the layout, and a moment later the affected pages reload in the browser. The watcher () wraps Python's library to observe filesystem events. It's structured as: 1. A watchdog observer running in a background thread, posting raw events to a queue. 2. A debounce buffer that aggregates events for 250ms (the default; configurable). Saving a file twice in quick succession only triggers one rebuild. 3. A dispatch callback that the dev server registers; the watcher calls it with the set of changed paths after the debounce window expires. The dispatch callback runs and then refreshes the metadata registry. If the build fails (e.g., a parser error), the watcher captures the exception and broadcasts it to the WebSocket overlay so the browser can display the error inline. Module cache invalidation When a file's Python half changes, the dev server's existing imported version of the compiled is stale. Python's import system caches modules in — reimporting the same module key returns the cached version. The watcher purges the cached module from after every rebuild, so the next request that needs it triggers a fresh import. Source: . This is why Python edits show up immediately without restarting the dev server. The hotreload story is: write file → watcher fires → incremental build → module cache purged → next request reads the new code. What's watched By default, the watcher observes: — recursive — recursive Any file referenced in or config The itself (changing the config triggers a full restart, not a hotreload) It does not watch , , , or any dotfiles. Those are either output directories (changing them would loop) or noise. Vite integration () supervises the Vite dev server subprocess. The Vite process is responsible for: Bundling JSX for the browser (with React Refresh / HMR) Serving static assets from Handling clientside hot module replacement when JSX files change Pyxle's dev server does not serve JS/CSS to the browser directly. Instead, it proxies asset requests to Vite's port. This sounds inefficient but it isn't: the proxy just forwards bytes, and Vite is the JS expert. Spawning Vite tries several command resolutions to find Vite: 1. — local install via npm 2. — local install, called via 3. — fallback if neither local install exists Each candidate is validated with before being committed to. If Vite isn't installed at all, Pyxle automatically runs to restore dependencies. (This is also why a fresh works on the second : the first attempt notices the missing dependencies and installs them.) Source: . Readiness probing After spawning Vite, Pyxle polls Vite's TCP port (default 5173) every 100ms until it accepts connections. Once it does, Vite is "ready" and Pyxle starts serving page requests. The whole readiness window is usually under a second. If Vite takes longer than 10 seconds to come up, Pyxle reports a timeout and shuts down — usually a sign that something else is holding port 5173. Autorestart If the Vite subprocess exits unexpectedly (crashes, OOMs, gets killed), Pyxle's monitor coroutine catches the exit and schedules a restart after 0.5 seconds. The restart probes for

searchIndex.title: Server-side rendering
searchIndex.description: Server-side rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a `PageRoute` and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robus
searchIndex.searchText: Serverside rendering Serverside rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robust, and predictable. It's also the part that touches both Python (the loader) and Node.js (the React renderer), so a fair bit of the complexity lives in the bridge between the two languages. This doc walks through the SSR pipeline stage by stage. By the end you'll know exactly what happens between "Starlette receives a GET" and "the browser hydrates", including the worker pool protocol, the head merge algorithm, and the streaming response strategy. Files (): File Lines What it does 720 Orchestrates loader, render, head, document assembly 310 Wraps Node.js component rendering (subprocess or worker pool) 330 Persistent SSR workers, NDJSON over stdin/stdout 420 Merges head elements from 4 sources, deduplicates, sanitizes 455 HTML document assembly (dev + production modes) 50 Reexports the public surface The interesting code lives in , , and . The rest is supporting infrastructure. The pipeline at a glance Source: . If anything goes wrong at any stage, error handling kicks in: A triggers the nearest boundary. A (the React component crashes during SSR) also tries the error boundary. An unexpected exception falls through to , which renders the developer overlay (in dev) or a generic page (in production). Stage 1: importing the compiled server module The page handler closure inside knows the for the current URL. The first thing it does is import the compiled Python module at : In dev mode, before importing, Pyxle purges any cached version of this module from : This is the hotreload mechanism. When you save a file: 1. The watcher rebuilds the artifacts. 2. The file on disk has new content. 3. The next request purges the old cached module. 4. Python's import machinery reads the new file from disk. 5. The new code runs. In production mode (), modules are imported once at startup and never reimported. This avoids the perrequest import overhead and is consistent with the immutable nature of a deployed build. Source: . Stage 2: running the loader Once the module is imported, Pyxle finds the function tagged with (set by the decorator) and calls it: Loaders are always async — the parser refuses to compile a sync . They take exactly one positional argument named (a Starlette object). They return a JSON serializable dict. A few invariants hold: The loader name comes from the metadata. Pyxle doesn't for "the function with " at runtime — it looked up the name once at compile time and stored it in . This means runtime startup doesn't have to walk the module looking for decorated functions. The loader is awaited directly. Pyxle does not wrap it in , doesn't add timeouts, doesn't catch exceptions outside of the documented error types. The loader is your code, and it runs as your code. The result must be JSONserializable. If you return a , a , or a custom class, the next stage () will raise . Pyxle catches that and routes it through the same error pipeline as any other render failure. If the loader raises , Pyxle catches it and looks for the nearest boundary up the directory tree. If found, it renders the error page with the error context as a prop. If not, it renders the default error document. If the loader raises any other exception, Pyxle treats it the same way but logs it as an unexpected failure and includes the stack trace in dev mode. Source: . Stage 3: resolving the head The of the HTML response is assembled from up to four sources, in order of increasing priority: 1. Layout JSX blocks — collected at registrybuild time from (and ) ancestors of the current page. 2. The page's variable — Python string, list of strings, or callable returning either. If it's a callable, Pyxle invokes it with the loader's data. 3. The page's JSX blocks — extracted from the page's JSX section by the parser at compile time. 4. Runtime registrations — rare; used by some advanced helpers that register head elements during SSR. Each source contributes a list of HTML strings (e.g. ). The function () merges them with deduplication and sanitization. Deduplication The merge identifies "the same" element by its deduplication key, which depends on the tag: Tag Dedupe key tag name (so only one survives) "charset" (singleton) "canonical" (singleton) (manual key) Anything else no key — always included If two elements share a key, the higherpriority one wins. So a in the page's JSX overrides a in the layout's JSX. A in the page's variable overrides one in the layout. Within the same priority tier, deeper nesting wins (page over parent layout, child layout over root layout). This matches the behaviour of React Helmet and other head libraries — your innermost layer is the most specific, so it should win. Source: . Sanitization Every element passes through (), which parses the element and rebuilds it through a strict allowlist: 1. Enforces a tag allowlist. Only , , , , and may appear in the head; anything else (, , , ...) is dropped entirely. 2. HTMLescapes every attribute value. Elements are rebuilt from their parsed attributes with values escaped, so a quote inside interpolated data can't break out of its attribute and inject markup. Trailing markup injected after a breakout is discarded. 3. Escapes and inside . The HTML spec says title content shouldn't contain raw HTML, but if a user ships dynamic title content from a loader, an attacker could try to inject a closing tag and break out of the title element. Pyxle escapes both characters defensively and discards anything after the closing . 4. Strips event handler attributes. Any attribute starting with (, , , etc.) is removed. Head elements don't run JavaScript, so these are pure XSS vectors. 5. Neutralizes , , and URLs in , , and attributes. A would actually execute that script in some browsers; Pyxle replaces the URL with an empty string. 6. Rejects — a datacontrolled refresh target is an openredirect vector. Void tags (, ) are reserialized selfclosing (), so output markup may differ cosmetically from the input. The sanitization is always on — there's no optout. Inline / content is the one deliberate passthrough: it is treated as trusted author code and preserved verbatim, so it must never contain interpolated user data. Stage 4: rendering the React component This is the most complex part of the SSR pipeline. We need to take a React component (which is JavaScript) and produce an HTML string from it (also JavaScript), all from inside Python. The mechanism is (), which delegates to either: Perrequest subprocess mode () — spawn a fresh Node.js subprocess for every render Worker pool mode (default, ) — keep N persistent Node.js workers alive Each mode has its own Node.js entry script: subprocess mode runs (one render per process), while the worker pool runs the longlived (a persistent render loop). Both share the same esbuild + React rendering core. The render protocol A render request is a JSON message: The Node.js worker: 1. Bundles the component with esbuild. Imports get inlined into a single JavaScript string. esbuild is fast — typically under 30ms even for nontrivial component trees. The bundle is cached per so repeat requests for the same page reuse the bundle. 2. Evaluates the bundle in a fresh context. The default export is the React component function. 3. Calls . This produces a plain HTML string from the component tree. (A request flagged instead drives and replies with a sequence of // frames sharing the request id — see Streaming SSR. The buffered path described here remains the default for every nonstreaming, cacheable, and statically generated render.) 4. Collects head elements emitted by blocks. When the component renders a block, the runtime captures the children and returns them separately (so they can be hoisted into the document head). 5. Replies with another JSON message. The reply: The field lets the worker pool match response
