searchIndex.title: Build and serve
searchIndex.description: `pyxle dev` is what you run during development. `pyxle build` and `pyxle serve` are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes:
searchIndex.searchText: Build and serve is what you run during development. and are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes: Aspect + Vite dev server on :5173 onetime bundle, then no Vite Source compilation incremental on file change full rebuild, all at once File watcher running not running HMR / React Refresh enabled disabled Module reloading perrequest purge imported once at startup Error responses full stack trace + dev overlay generic Asset serving proxy to Vite static files from Route discovery metadata files in Port 8000 by default 8000 by default This doc explains the production pipeline: what does, what artifacts it produces, and how runs them. Files (): File Lines What it does 343 The orchestrator 193 Invokes and parses its output 26 Loads 7 Reexports The CLI commands themselves live in : is around line 400, is around line 517. What actually does When you run , the pipeline executes six steps in order: Source: . The directory is the only thing your deployment needs. Once it exists, you can it on a server, copy it to a container image, push it to a CDN, or do anything else you'd do with a staticplusserver build. Step 1: Compile sources Steps 1 of is identical to the initial build of — same function, same calls, same layout. The compiler doesn't know or care that we're in production mode; it just produces artifacts. The result is the same three files per page in : Plus any layoutcomposed route modules in and any files copied to . If anything fails to compile (a parser error, an unresolved import, a missing decorator, etc.), the build aborts here and prints the error. Production builds are strict — there is no tolerant mode for . The first error stops everything. Step 2: Run Vite build () invokes Vite to bundle the clientside JavaScript. It tries multiple invocation strategies in order: 1. if has a script. 2. Local if installed. 3. as a fallback. 4. if no local install. Each candidate is validated with before use. If no Vite is available at all, the pipeline runs to restore dependencies, then retries. The Vite invocation passes: — Pyxle's autogenerated Vite config — produce a manifest JSON that maps source files to hashed bundle entries env var — sets the asset base path so Vite emits URLs like instead of Vite's output: — the manifest — bundled, codesplit, hashed JS — bundled, hashed CSS — Vite's default HTML output (we ignore this; Pyxle generates its own HTML at request time) Vite logs are streamed to the console with a prefix. If the exit code is nonzero, Pyxle raises with the captured stderr. Step 3: Load Vite's manifest Vite's manifest looks like this: Each entry tells you the bundled file path, the list of imported chunks (so you can preload them), and the direct CSS dependencies. The interesting part is the import chain. imports , which itself has CSS. To get the complete CSS list for , we need to walk the imports recursively and collect all arrays. Otherwise we'd ship pages with missing styles from shared modules. () does this walk. It uses a visited set to handle cycles (Vite shouldn't produce cycles, but defense in depth) and returns the deduplicated list of CSS files for each entry. Step 4: Build the page manifest Vite's manifest is keyed by source file. Pyxle's is keyed by route: () iterates the metadata registry, looks up each page's bundled assets in the Vite manifest, walks the import chain to collect CSS, and emits one entry per route. Aliases (from optional catchalls) get their own entry pointing at the same data: The page manifest is written to and is the source of truth for production routing. The dev server's metadata registry is built from ; the production server's metadata registry is built from this single file. Step 5: Copy into The final layout under is: The and directories are direct copies of and . The directory is Vite's output () copied verbatim. is a copy of your project's directory (if it exists). These are static assets that ship to the browser unchanged: favicons, images, robots.txt, etc. The double nesting is intentional — Vite's output naturally lives under a subdirectory of its base path, and Pyxle preserves that. The serving layer is configured to mount it at the right URL prefix (). What does Once you have a directory, runs it. The serve command: 1. Loads with . This is the single most important setting that flips the framework into production mode. 2. Loads as the route source. 3. Builds a from the manifest entries. 4. Creates a from the registry. 5. Spawns the SSR worker pool. Same code as dev mode. 6. Builds a Starlette app with the same factory. The factory checks and includes the (productiononly) and skips the Vite proxy middleware (which would have nothing to talk to). 7. Runs uvicorn to serve the Starlette app. Source: . The result is a process that: Listens on port 8000 (or whatever you pass). Imports each compiled module once at startup, not perrequest. Serves , , and routes via mounts. Handles page routes the same way does — same SSR pipeline, same loader execution, same component rendering, same head merging. Responds to errors with opaque generic pages instead of the developer overlay. Why is the SSR worker pool the same in production? Because the rendering work doesn't change between dev and prod. The React component is the same JavaScript. esbuild bundles it the same way. produces the same output. The cost of running React on the server is identical. The only thing that changes is how often you pay for it: in dev, the worker is mostly idle and serves your singledeveloper requests. In production, the worker pool needs to scale to handle real traffic — which is why the recommended worker count for production is the number of CPU cores, not 1. You can adjust: …to spin up four persistent Node.js workers. Roundrobin dispatch gives you fourway SSR parallelism within one Pyxle process. Stateless processes, scale horizontally Pyxle's serve process is stateless: nothing is stored inprocess between requests. This means you can run multiple Pyxle instances behind a load balancer and they'll all serve the same content. The scaling story is "run more processes" — not "run more threads" or "tune a connection pool." Configuration overrides for production builds the production config with three overrides: Source: . is the critical one. It turns off: The hotreload purge The dev error overlay (replaced with opaque generic responses) The Vite client tag in the document The React Refresh preamble The WebSocket overlay endpoint It also turns on: GZip middleware Production asset path resolution via Streaming responses use the production document shell The full list of what controls is scattered across , , and — search the codebase for to see every gate. Other CLI flags for — bind port (default 8000) — bind host (default 127.0.0.1; use 0.0.0.0 for "listen on all interfaces", which you'll want behind a reverse proxy) — where to read the build output from (default: in the current directory) — skip the implicit and use the existing asis. Useful when the build artifacts come from CI and you just want to run them. — force a fresh build before serving (the default) — whether to serve and directly. Disable this if you're putting Pyxle behind a CDN that handles static assets. — number of persistent Node.js workers (default 1) What's in a deployable artifact? If you want to deploy a Pyxle app, the artifact is the directory plus the Python source for any files outside (your shared utilities, your dependencies, your ). A typical deployment looks like: The deployment process: 1. On the build machine: 2. Copy , , and the Python source to the server (or build a container image). 3. On the server: . Node.js isn't required for serving unless you have SSR workers spawning... wait, scratch that. Node.js is required — the SSR pipeline runs React on Node.js workers. searchIndex.title: The runtime
searchIndex.description: The "runtime" of Pyxle is the **smallest module in the framework**. It contains two decorators (`@server` and `@action`), two exception classes (`LoaderError` and `ActionError`), and one small helper
searchIndex.searchText: The runtime The "runtime" of Pyxle is the smallest module in the framework. It contains two decorators ( and ), two exception classes ( and ), and one small helper () — and nothing else. The whole file is about 150 lines including blank lines and docstrings. This is on purpose. The runtime is what your application code imports — it's the contract between your code and the framework. A small contract means a small surface area for bugs, a small mental model for users, and a small attack surface for prompt injection through documentation. This doc is the shortest in the architecture section because there isn't much code to explain. But the design decisions behind the runtime are some of the most important in Pyxle, and they're worth understanding. File: (150 lines) What's in The core contract is small enough to reproduce here: That's the core. The one remaining export is — a small helper that tags a response with an header so the client router drops its cached navigation payloads for the given URLs after a mutation (see Server Actions). Two decorators, two exceptions, one helper, one . Why so small? Most web frameworks have a runtime that's enormous: a request context, a global state container, dependency injection, an event loop, middleware execution, response builders, error handlers, the works. Pyxle has none of those things in its runtime. The reason is the compiler does all the framework work, not the runtime. By the time your code runs at request time: The parser has already extracted your loader and action metadata. The compiler has already injected the runtime imports into the artifact. The dev server has already imported the compiled module. Starlette has already dispatched the request. The SSR pipeline has already validated everything. When the framework finally calls your decorated function, the function is just a function. There is no context manager wrapping it, no try/except catching its exceptions, no proxy object intercepting attribute access. It runs as if you'd called it yourself. This is the nomagic design principle from the architecture README, applied as literally as possible. What actually does Here's the entire implementation of : That's the whole decorator. It sets one attribute on the function and returns it. The function is the same function — same identity, same signature, same , same , same , same closure cells, same everything. You can call it directly without going through Pyxle: The framework's only use of is at parse time, in the compiler — and even then, the compiler reads it from the AST (by checking decorator names), not from the runtime. The attribute is set as a documentation aid for tooling and debuggers, not as a mechanism the framework relies on. works identically, with instead. Why decorators at all then? If doesn't do anything, why is it a decorator? Why not just a naming convention like Flask's ? Because the parser uses the decorator name to find loaders and actions in the AST. When the parser walks your file, it asks each function definition: "is one of your decorators called ?" If yes, it treats the function as a loader. Same for . Naming conventions () would work, but they have downsides: Multiple functions per file would conflict. If the convention is "the function named is the loader", you can't have helper functions named without confusion. With a decorator, you can name the function whatever you want. You'd lose the explicit "this is a Pyxle thing" signal. When you read someone else's code, immediately tells you "this is the framework's entry point". A naming convention is invisible. Refactoring tools can't track names as reliably as decorators. Renaming a function is easy; changing a magic name convention isn't. The decorator wins on every axis except one: it requires an explicit , and even that is autoinjected by the compiler so you don't actually have to write the import yourself. Pyxle in plain Python: You can write the import yourself. The compiler checks for an existing and skips the autoinjection if you've already added it. The autoinjection exists because most users won't bother, not because the framework rejects explicit imports. The exception types and are the only structured errors the runtime defines. Both follow the same shape: You raise them from inside your loader or action when something is wrong: When is raised: The framework catches it. It looks for the nearest boundary. It renders the boundary with the error context as a prop: . It returns the response with HTTP status . works the same way for functions, except it returns a JSON error response () instead of rendering an HTML boundary. The default differs: defaults to 500 because most loader failures are serverside bugs. defaults to 400 because most action failures are clientside input problems. You override the defaults with the keyword argument: Why two exception types? Because they're caught at different points in the request pipeline, they translate to different response shapes (HTML page vs JSON), and they trigger different debugging affordances (error boundary vs clientside error handler). A single would force the framework to disambiguate at catch time, and would force users to remember which fields apply to which context. Two types make the contract explicit. The zerodependency rule has zero imports from the rest of the framework. The only things it imports are: (a Python 3.x compatibility thing) (standard library) No , no , no , nothing. This is enforced by rule 5 (the module boundary rule). The reason is runtime.py is the only module that ends up imported by your application code. When you write: …you're pulling into your project's import graph. If runtime.py imported , your project would transitively depend on the parser, the AST module, the JSX import rewriter, the Babel subprocess wrapper, and so on. That's a huge dependency footprint for a module that ends up being five lines of metadatasetting code. The zerodependency rule keeps the runtime fast to import and easy to test. You can import in 5 milliseconds. You can test it without setting up a full Pyxle project. You can use it from nonPyxle code if you wanted to, just to mark functions for some other framework's use. How the framework actually finds your loader Given that the runtime decorators don't do anything at runtime, how does the framework find your loader when a request comes in? The answer: at parse time, not at runtime. When the parser processes , walks the Python AST looking for nodes whose contains a named or an ending in . The first match becomes the loader. Its name is recorded in the dataclass: This metadata flows into the (compiled), then into the artifact, then into the at startup, then into the dataclass. At request time, the page handler does: It looks up the loader by name. The name was recorded by the parser at compile time. The decorator's role is naming the metadata key, not modifying behaviour. This is why the runtime can be three lines per decorator: by the time the function runs, the framework already knows everything it needs to know about it. The decorator doesn't have to do work because the parser already did it. What if I need real request middleware? If you need to wrap loaders with crosscutting behaviour (auth, logging, rate limiting, etc.), Pyxle has two options: 1. Custom middleware — Starlette ASGI middleware declared in : This is the right place for things that should run on every request regardless of which loader is invoked. 2. Route hooks — Perroute policies declared in : This is the right place for things that should run for most page or API routes but not all of them. Both mechanisms wrap the request outside the loader, so the loader itself stays untouched. The runtime decorators don't have to participate. Source: , . What if I want to test my loader? Because doesn't wrap the function, testing is trivial: You don't need a test client, you don't need a fake Starlette app, you don't need to mock the request context. The loader is

searchIndex.searchText: The CLI is the commandline entry point users actually type. It's a Typer application with a small set of singlepurpose commands: Command Purpose Scaffold a new Pyxle project Install Python + Node dependencies Run the development server Compile and bundle for production Serve a production build Validate the project without serving Print the filebased route table TypeScriptcheck compiled JSX This doc explains what each command does, how flags and configuration flow from user input to the rest of the framework, and the design choices behind the CLI surface. Files: (1220 lines) — the Typer commands (115 lines) — scaffolding (100 lines) — copies template files (50 lines) — template registry (190 lines) — with structured output (500 lines) — schema and validation Configuration precedence Every Pyxle command reads configuration in the same order, from lowest to highest priority: 1. Builtin defaults — Hardcoded in field defaults (). 2. — JSON file at the project root. 3. Environment variables — Variables starting with override the corresponding config field. 4. CLI flags — Anything you pass on the command line wins. Higher priority overrides lower priority. So: …ends up running on port 8001 (CLI beats env beats file beats default). The mechanism is (), which returns a new frozen instance with selective field updates. Each layer applies overrides on top of the previous one. The CLI layer runs last: ignores values, so passing overrides while not passing leaves the lower layer alone. This is the standard "explicit wins, implicit yields" pattern. Source: . Scaffolds a new Pyxle project from a template. Under the hood: 1. Validates the name. Must be a nonempty string that's safe to use as a directory name. The directory must not exist (unless is passed). 2. Resolves the template. Defaults to . Templates live in inside the package — they're shipped with . 3. Copies the template files into the new directory using . The template includes: — a working "Hello, Pyxle" page — a sample API route — Vite + React 18 + Tailwind dependencies — minimal config () — pinned version , — Tailwind setup — favicon and a few static files — sensible defaults 4. Optionally installs dependencies. With (the default), runs and in the new directory. With , skips both — useful for CI or when you want to inspect the scaffold before installing. Source: , , . Why does the scaffold ship with the framework? A few alternatives we considered: Download from a Git repository. Requires the user to have network access and installed. Doesn't pin a version. Download from a CDN. Requires the user to have network access. Versions can drift. Generate from a template engine. Adds a runtime dependency on Jinja2 or similar. Templates become harder to read and modify. Shipping the scaffold as part of the Pyxle package means: It works offline. It's pinned to the framework version automatically. You can read it directly: to find the install path, then look at . You can fork it locally without affecting the upstream package. The downside is that updating the scaffold requires a Pyxle release, not a separate publish step. We think the tradeoff is worth it for betastage software where the install story matters more than ergonomics. Runs the development server. The full lifecycle is documented in The dev server, but the CLIspecific bits are: Flags Flag Default Effect Starlette bind host Starlette bind port Vite bind host Vite bind port Dev mode toggle Config file path from config Override SSR worker count auto Force/disable Tailwind watcher What it does 1. Resolves the project root (CLI argument or current directory). 2. Loads , applies env vars, applies CLI flags. 3. Resolves global stylesheets and scripts via the styling helpers. 4. Builds a from the merged config. 5. Creates a instance and calls . The actual heavy lifting happens inside — spawning Vite, starting the SSR worker pool, building the Starlette app, starting the file watcher, running uvicorn. See The dev server. Source: . Tailwind handling Pyxle has optional firstclass Tailwind support. The CLI autodetects Tailwind by checking for or in the project root. If found: A separate Tailwind watcher process is spawned alongside Vite. The watcher rebuilds the Tailwind CSS file whenever a Tailwind source class changes. When also exists, the standalone Tailwind watcher is skipped — Vite handles Tailwind via PostCSS instead, which is faster and integrates with HMR. The flag lets you force one mode or the other. Most users never touch it. Source: , . Compiles and bundles the project for production. Detailed walkthrough in Build and serve. Flags Flag Default Effect Config file path (full rebuild) Skip unchanged files Output directory What it does 1. Loads config, applies env, applies CLI flags. 2. Builds a (yes, the same dataclass uses — reuses 100% of the dev server's settings structure). 3. Lazily imports (lazy because importing the build pipeline pulls in , , etc., and we want and to start fast). 4. Calls . 5. Prints a summary: pages, API routes, client assets. Source: . The lazy import pattern You'll notice uses lazy imports for the heavy modules: This is intentional. should not have to load the build pipeline. should not have to load Starlette. By deferring imports until they're actually needed, the CLI startup stays fast — typically under 100ms for or , even on cold imports. This pattern is documented in rule 16 (Lazy imports for heavy modules). Serves a production build. See Build and serve for the full walkthrough. Flags Flag Default Effect Starlette bind host Starlette bind port Config file path Where to read the build from Skip the implicit Serve and from config Override SSR worker count What it does 1. Loads config, applies env, applies CLI flags. 2. Forces in the resolved config. This is the single most important production override. 3. Optionally runs first (the default — set to use existing artifacts). 4. Loads to populate the route registry. 5. Builds the Starlette app via (same factory as ). 6. Spawns uvicorn to serve the app. Source: . is for CI In a typical CI pipeline: Then, on the production server: tells Pyxle "the artifacts are already in , don't rebuild them." This separates "compile time" from "run time" cleanly. In dev, you usually omit the flag and let build for you. Validates the project without starting a server. This is the linter / precommit hook command. What it checks 1. The project structure exists. must be a directory. 2. The config is valid. (if present) must parse and pass schema validation. 3. Node.js dependencies are present. should exist (warning if missing). 4. Every file parses cleanly. Both Python and JSX halves. Tolerant mode The fourth check is the interesting one. runs the parser in tolerant mode: Source: . Tolerant mode means the parser collects every diagnostic in every file in a single pass instead of stopping at the first error. This is the right behaviour for a linter — you want to see all your errors at once, not one error at a time. adds Babelbacked JSX validation on top, so JSX syntax errors (unclosed tags, mismatched braces, invalid expressions) are also caught. This is the only Pyxle command that runs Babel validation by default; and skip it because Vite catches JSX errors at bundle time. Defensive perfile wrapping Each perfile parse is wrapped in a : This is defenseindepth. Tolerant mode shouldn't raise — it collects errors instead — but a future parser bug could throw an unexpected exception. The defensive wrap catches it, reports it as a structured diagnostic, and continues scanning the rest of the project. This was added during the parser audit on 20260408 after a pathological fixture (200level nested expression) was found to crash CPython's parser stack and abort the entire run midscan. With the wrap, a single broken file is reported and the scan continues. Source: . Audit details: , "Bug 3". Cascade suppression runs both the Python parser AND Babel for every file. searchIndex.title: SSR Pipeline
searchIndex.description: This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance.
searchIndex.searchText: SSR Pipeline This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance. Overview When a request hits a page route, Pyxle: 1. Resolves the route matches the URL to a file 2. Imports the server module the compiled Python code from the section 3. Runs the loader executes the function with the request 4. Resolves HEAD elements evaluates the variable (static or dynamic) 5. Renders the component runs React serverside rendering via Node.js 6. Merges head elements deduplicates and sanitises head elements from all sources 7. Assembles the document builds the HTML document with hydration scripts 8. Streams the response sends the HTML to the client Component rendering The React component is rendered serverside using Node.js. Pyxle supports two rendering modes: Subprocess mode (default when workers = 0) Each render spawns a new Node.js process: 1. esbuild bundles the component and its imports into a single file 2. Node.js executes the bundle with from 3. The rendered HTML and any extracted head elements are returned as JSON 4. The subprocess exits This mode is simple but has startup overhead (200400ms per request). Worker pool mode (default: 1 worker) Persistent Node.js processes eliminate startup cost: 1. On server start, Pyxle launches N worker processes 2. Workers stay running, communicating via stdin/stdout with newlinedelimited JSON 3. Each render sends a request to an available worker 4. The worker bundles the component with esbuild, renders it, and returns the result 5. Latency drops to 3080ms (esbuild bundling only) Configure workers: Head element pipeline Head elements come from three sources, listed in order of increasing priority: 1. Layout blocks from files up the directory tree 2. Page variable from the Python section of the page's file 3. Page blocks from components in the page's JSX The merge process: 1. All sources are split into individual elements using 2. Each element is sanitised (event handlers stripped, title content escaped, dangerous URLs removed) 3. Elements are deduplicated by tagspecific keys (title by tag name, meta by name/property, etc.) 4. Higherpriority sources override lowerpriority ones for duplicate keys 5. Nondeduplicatable elements (inline scripts, custom tags) are all included Document assembly The final HTML document is structured as: In dev mode, the document includes the Vite client script and React Refresh preamble for hot module replacement. Streaming For production builds with a manifest, Pyxle uses to send the document in chunks: 1. The HTML prefix (up to ) is sent immediately 2. The serverrendered body HTML follows 3. The suffix (hydration scripts and closing tags) completes the stream This allows the browser to start parsing and rendering before the full document is ready. Error handling Errors at each stage are handled differently: Stage Error type Behaviour Loader Renders nearest with error context Loader Other exceptions Renders or default error document HEAD evaluation Renders or default error document Component render Renders or default error document In dev mode, the error overlay shows the error with breadcrumbs indicating which stage failed. Clientside navigation When navigating between pages clientside, Pyxle requests a JSON payload for the same URL instead of full HTML — signalled by a request header, not a query param: This returns: The client runtime swaps the component props and updates the document head without a full page reload. SSR worker locale Since 0.3.0 the Node SSR worker pins its locale deterministically so , , , and similar APIs produce the same strings on every host. Without this, the worker inherits whatever the process was started with — often on CI, on EU servers, in containers — and any serverrendered formatted date immediately mismatches what the browser produces, tripping React hydration. Default: , . Override both via the environment variable before Pyxle boots: The worker only sets these if the surrounding environment hasn't already — so a systemd unit or container image that pins its own still wins. If a page genuinely needs userlocaleaware formatting, render those pieces inside instead; SSR emits a placeholder and the client fills it in after mount, avoiding the mismatch entirely. Performance tips Use worker pool mode () for production. Start with N = number of CPU cores. Keep loader functions fast. The loader runs on every request cache expensive calls. Avoid heavy imports in the Python section. Imports are loaded every time the module is hotreloaded in dev mode. Minimise HEAD callable complexity. Dynamic HEAD functions run synchronously on the main thread.

searchIndex.title: Compiler Internals
searchIndex.description: The Pyxle compiler transforms `.pyxl` files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works.
searchIndex.searchText: Compiler Internals The Pyxle compiler transforms files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works. Compilation overview When you run or , the compiler: 1. Scans the directory for files 2. Parses each file to separate Python from JSX 3. Writes serverside Python modules to 4. Writes clientside JSX modules to 5. Generates layout composition wrappers in 6. Creates a Vite configuration at The parser The parser () is ASTdriven — there are no fence markers, string directives, or perline keyword heuristics. It finds the Python/JSX boundary by walking the source and, at each position, growing the largest region that parses as valid Python via ; when Python stops parsing, it grows a JSX segment until valid Python resumes. How the split works The boundary is decided by what parses as Python, not by the leading keyword. is not valid Python, so it lands in JSX; is, so it stays in Python. Arbitrary alternation is supported (), including JSXfirst files. Inside a JSX segment the parser tracks JS structural state (string and template literals, block comments, brace/paren/bracket depth), so Pythonlooking text inside a JSX body or template literal is never misclassified. What the parser extracts Artifact Description All Python lines concatenated All JSX lines concatenated function metadata (name, line number, parameters) function metadata (name, line number, parameters) Static variable content Whether is a callable JSX blocks extracted for serverside use component props component props Validation The parser enforces (raising a compiletime error otherwise): At most one loader per file and functions must be / must be defined at module scope (not nested) Their first parameter must be named function names must be unique within the file Code generation Server module () The compiled Python module contains: Client module () The compiled JSX module contains: Composed route module () When layouts exist, the compiler generates a wrapper: Vite configuration The compiler generates that: Configures for JSX transforms and React Refresh Sets the to the build directory Maps import aliases for Injects environment variables via Vite's option Incremental compilation During , the file watcher triggers recompilation only for changed files: 1. The watcher detects a file change in 2. Only the changed file is recompiled 3. The server module is reimported (with module cache invalidation) 4. Vite's HMR picks up the clientside changes automatically Build artifacts After , the output structure is: The maps each route to its clientside assets:

searchIndex.title: Frequently Asked Questions
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in `.pyxl` files, providing file-based routing, server-side rendering, and a development experie
searchIndex.searchText: Frequently Asked Questions General What is Pyxle? Pyxle is a Pythonfirst fullstack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in files, providing filebased routing, serverside rendering, and a development experience inspired by Next.js. How is Pyxle different from Next.js? Next.js uses JavaScript/TypeScript for both server and client code. Pyxle uses Python for server logic (data fetching, mutations, API routes) and React for the UI. If your team prefers Python for backend work but wants React for the frontend, Pyxle bridges the gap. How is Pyxle different from Django or Flask? Django and Flask are backend frameworks that render templates. Pyxle is a fullstack framework that: Uses React for the UI instead of template engines Provides serverside rendering (SSR) with clientside hydration Colocates server logic and UI in the same file Includes a bundler (Vite) for hot reload and optimised builds Offers filebased routing instead of URL configuration How does Pyxle compare to Reflex, NiceGUI, or Streamlit? Those frameworks let you build the UI in pure Python; Pyxle has you write real React/JSX — so the entire npm React ecosystem is available — colocated with your Python. The honest tradeoffs, and when each of them is the better choice, are laid out in the full framework comparison. Is Pyxle productionready? Pyxle is in beta (version 0.4.0). The core features are implemented and tested (1,370+ tests, with a 95% coverage gate enforced in CI), but the API may change before 1.0. Use it for new projects and experiments, but be prepared for breaking changes. What Python version do I need? Python 3.10 or later. Python 3.12 is recommended. What Node.js version do I need? Node.js 18 or later. Node.js 20+ is recommended. files Can I use TypeScript in files? The JSX section supports JSX syntax. TypeScript type annotations are not directly supported in files, but you can run to typecheck the compiled JSX output if you have TypeScript installed. Can I import Python code in the JSX section? No. The Python and JSX sections are compiled into separate files. Python code runs on the server; JSX runs on both server (SSR) and client. Data flows from Python to JSX through the loader's return value. Can I use any React library? Yes. Any npm package that works with React 18 and Vite should work. Install it via and import it in your JSX section. Can I have multiple loaders in one file? No. Only one function per file. You can have multiple functions. Routing How do I create a 404 page? Create : How do I create a catchall route? Use : How do I exclude a folder from routing? Wrap it in parentheses: creates the route , not . Data loading My loader is slow. Can I cache results? Pyxle does not include builtin caching. Use your own caching strategy: For async caching, consider libraries like . Can I access the database from a loader? Yes. Loaders are standard async Python functions. Use any async database library (asyncpg, databases, SQLAlchemy async, Tortoise ORM, etc.). What happens if my loader throws an unhandled exception? If you raise , the nearest is rendered. Other exceptions render a default error page. In dev mode, the error overlay shows the full stack trace. Styling Do I have to use Tailwind CSS? No. Tailwind is included in the scaffold for convenience, but you can remove it and use any CSS approach: plain CSS files, CSS Modules, Sass, CSSinJS libraries, etc. How do I add a global CSS reset? Add it to the config: Or link it in your root layout's : Deployment How do I deploy Pyxle? 1. Run to compile assets 2. Run to start the production server 3. Place behind a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy) for TLS See Deployment for Docker examples and detailed instructions. Can I deploy to serverless platforms? Pyxle requires a persistent server for SSR (Node.js workers). It is best suited for containerbased deployments (Docker, Kubernetes) or VPS hosting. Serverless platforms that support longrunning processes may work, but this is not tested. How do I set environment variables in production? Use environment variables or a file: Security Is CSRF protection enabled by default? Yes. The doublesubmit cookie pattern is active for all , , , and requests. The component and hook handle tokens automatically. How do I disable CSRF for webhooks? Exempt specific paths: Are my environment variables safe? Variables without the prefix never appear in clientside code. Only variables are injected into JavaScript bundles. Troubleshooting fails with "npm install required" Run or in your project directory. The page renders without styles compiles Tailwind through PostCSS automatically when is present (the scaffold default). If you removed that file or are on a custom stylesheet setup, recheck your styling configuration — specifically that the relevant CSS is imported from a page or layout. Hot reload is not working 1. Check that Vite is running (look for the Vite URL in the terminal output) 2. Check the browser console for WebSocket connection errors 3. Try restarting SSR fails with "Unable to load page module" The compiled Python module could not be imported. Check: 1. Syntax errors in the Python section of your file 2. Missing Python dependencies (run ) 3. Import errors in your function Actions return 403 Forbidden CSRF token mismatch. If you are making manual fetch calls (not using or ), include the CSRF token header. See Security.

searchIndex.description: Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (`0.x`), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run `pip install --upgrade pyxle-framework`.
searchIndex.searchText: Changelog Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run . Unreleased Background tasks & deferred work. Two ways to run work off the request's critical path. Inside an , (a Starlette ) runs after the response is sent — ideal for "send the welcome email after signup"; returning is shorthand for a single such task. For fireandforget work from anywhere (loaders, actions, API routes), the new schedules it on a small inprocess worker pool — coroutine functions are awaited, plain callables run in a thread, a failing task is logged (never takes a worker down), and the queue is bounded ( under overload). The queue starts/stops with the app. Multiworker caveat: it's perprocess (like the inprocess WebSocket broker), so for durability or crossworker/exactlyonce semantics, hand off to a real job queue — the guide documents Celery, ARQ, and Dramatiq integration. See Background Tasks. OpenTelemetry tracing (optin). Emit OpenTelemetry spans for each request plus child spans for the SSR render, loaders, and calls, so a slow page surfaces as a drilldown trace. It's the heaviest integration, so it's a separate optional extra () and fully off by default — with the extra absent, spans are a zerocost noop (one boolean check), and the exporter endpoint comes from the standard env var. Sampling defaults to 5%. A base Pyxle install pulls in no OpenTelemetry packages; enabling without the extra fails loudly at startup. See Observability → OpenTelemetry tracing. — a live terminal observability panel. Opt into a compact panel that refreshes every few seconds with request throughput and error rate, statusclass breakdown, request/SSRrender/loader/action latency, and the pagecache hit ratio. It reads the same inprocess metrics (so it's free on the request path), is dependencyfree (no ), and is devonly — in production you'd scrape the metrics endpoint instead. See Observability → Dev dashboard. Structured access logging. Opt into one structured log line per request — , , , , and the request's correlation id — via , in (humanreadable) or format. The correlation id is bound into a context variable for the request's duration, so your own log calls pick it up too. Works with no new dependency (a stdlib JSON/console formatter); the optional extra adds structlog for richer rendering. Off by default so it won't disrupt an existing log pipeline. See Observability → Structured logging. Observability: request IDs, timing, metrics, and richer health probes. Every request now gets a correlation id — surfaced as the response header and on , readable from any loader or — and its duration is measured, along with SSRrender, loader, action, and pagecache (hit/stale/miss) metrics. The requestid and timing are on by default (a and two reads, no I/O, added at the outermost layer so even a CSRF rejection is tagged); an incoming is not trusted unless you opt in (), since echoing a client value into logs is a spoofing vector. Metrics are recorded into a bounded, allocationcheap inprocess registry and exposed — optin — at a Prometheus endpoint (, optionally bearerguarded); the pagecache hit ratio comes from the existing server cache. now runs dependency checks (today: the SSR worker pool has a live worker) and returns until the server is genuinely ready, while stays a pure liveness probe. All configured under the new [](reference/configuration.md#observability) block ( disables requestid/timing); the helper reads the id anywhere. Multiworker note: metrics are perworker (each series carries a label) — aggregate at the scraper. Structured logging, a dashboard, and OpenTelemetry land next in Phase 2.7. See Observability. Typed request validation for with Pydantic. An action can now declare a body parameter annotated with a Pydantic model — — and Pyxle parses, coerces, and validates the request body before the action runs, passing in a fullytyped instance. No more handwritten + keybykey checks. On failure the action is not called and the client gets a with a toplevel map (field path → messages, dotted for nested models and indexed for list items: , ). Both and surface it — / the reactive , and — so you can render messages next to each input. For checks Pydantic can't express (uniqueness, crossfield rules) raise the new , which is autoimported alongside and produces the same shape. Because the body models are real Pydantic models, generates an OpenAPI 3.1 document of every action's request body straight from your code (), derived from runtime introspection so it always matches what the dispatcher validates. Pydantic is an optional dependency — install the extra with ; actions without a model parameter are unaffected, and an action that declares one without Pydantic installed fails with a clear error rather than silently skipping validation. The generated / runtimes now also ship TypeScript declarations (). See Server Actions → Validating request bodies. Fix: a server module that fails to import is no longer cached as a broken empty module. If a page's compiled server module raised during its first import (e.g. a bad toplevel ), the halfinitialised module was left in , so every later request for that page silently got an empty module instead of reraising the real error. Failed imports are now evicted, so the error surfaces consistently on each request. WebSockets — page handlers, a client hook, and pub/sub. A page can now export a modulescope and Pyxle serves a WebSocket route at the page's path, alongside its HTTP render — the same file handles both protocols, and dynamic segments () resolve into . Detection is by convention (the function name, not a decorator), matching how modules already expose a callable. The new module ships a small pub/sub layer: subscribes a connection to a named channel and broadcasts to everyone in it, with staleconnection pruning. Because Starlette runs HTTP middleware only for scope, a WS handler gets no middleware — so also provides (resolve the pyxleauth session from the handshake cookie) and (the WS equivalent of CSRF). On the client, the generated hook gives with JSON parsing and exponentialbackoff reconnect, and never opens a socket during SSR. Multiworker caveat (called out honestly): the default inprocess broker lives in one process, so under a message published on one worker isn't seen by clients on another — Pyxle logs a warning when it detects this, and the protocol lets a Redisbacked broker drop in. See WebSockets. Streaming SSR for pages. A page that wraps a slow part in a React 18 boundary now streams: Pyxle flushes the shell (everything outside the boundary) immediately for a fast timetofirstbyte, then streams each boundary's content in as it resolves. It's optin and zeroconfig — the compiler detects and only those pages stream; every other page keeps the buffered render byteforbyte. The page hydrates exactly as before (the browser hydrates the same component), so no app changes are needed beyond adding the boundary. Streaming applies to dynamic, perrequest pages only: a publicly cacheable route ( envelope, directive, or edge entry) still renders buffered so it can be stored and 'd, and a streamed response is always . The document head is flushed before the component renders, so a streamed page uses its static (a runtimerendered lands too late — buffered pages are unaffected). A render error before the first byte falls back to the nearest rather than emitting a partial document. Built on the Node worker's new multiframe transport. See Streaming SSR. routelevel loading states. A file declares a loading component that wraps the whole route in — streamed as the shell on the server and applied identically on the client so hydration matches. The nearest wins (directory walkup, like ), and it composes with a page's own inner . Honest caveat: because the loader runs before render, the fallback only appears when the render suspends (a child using , , or a thrown promise) —
